apt.body:
# BlueNoroff — APT Profile

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 5 independent sources

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | North Korea — RGB (Lazarus financial subgroup / APT38) |
| Aliases | APT38, Stardust Chollima, Sapphire Sleet, TA444, BeagleBoyz, NICKEL GLADSTONE |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0082 |
| First seen | 2014 |
| Status | Active (DPRK's primary financial-theft unit) |
| Motivation | Financial (regime revenue); some espionage support |

## Overview
BlueNoroff is the financially motivated North Korean threat actor tracked by MITRE as **APT38 (G0082)** and widely reported under the name BlueNoroff (Kaspersky), Sapphire Sleet (Microsoft), and TA444 (Proofpoint). It operates as the money-making arm of the broader Lazarus umbrella under the RGB. Where Lazarus and Kimsuky pursue mixed espionage/financial goals, BlueNoroff is laser-focused on stealing money: it pioneered the SWIFT bank heists (including the 2016 Bangladesh Bank theft), ATM cash-out schemes (the "FASTCash"/BeagleBoyz operations), and — in recent years — large-scale cryptocurrency theft from exchanges, DeFi platforms, and individual blockchain professionals.

## Attribution & Origin
APT38/BlueNoroff is attributed to North Korea's Reconnaissance General Bureau. The U.S. CISA/FBI/Treasury "HIDDEN COBRA" and "BeagleBoyz" advisories, OFAC sanctions, and vendor reporting (Kaspersky, Mandiant, Microsoft, Proofpoint, Kaspersky's GReAT/Securelist) all attribute its financial operations to the DPRK. It shares code, infrastructure, and tradecraft with the wider Lazarus cluster but is consistently distinguished by its bank/crypto-theft mission.

## Motivation & Objectives
- **Financial (overwhelming):** generate hard currency for the sanctioned DPRK regime via bank fraud, ATM cash-outs, and cryptocurrency theft.
- **Espionage (supporting):** reconnaissance of financial institutions, SWIFT environments, and crypto/Web3 organizations to enable theft.

## Target Profile
BlueNoroff targets the financial ecosystem worldwide: cryptocurrency exchanges, blockchain/Web3 startups, venture capital firms, fintechs, banks, SWIFT-connected institutions, and ATM networks. Its **SnatchCrypto** and **GhostCall** operations heavily target blockchain developers, founders, and C-level/VC executives. **APAC / Australia & financial/crypto relevance is very high:** BlueNoroff has hit financial institutions and crypto firms across the Asia-Pacific, Middle East, and globally. Australian and APAC crypto exchanges, Web3 startups, and the VCs that fund them are direct targets — the group approaches victims via Telegram/LinkedIn with fake investment meetings and fake-Zoom/Microsoft Teams lures, increasingly on **macOS** (executives' machines). Regional fintech and exchange security teams should treat unsolicited "investor call" and meeting-software-update prompts as high-risk indicators.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK

### Initial Access
- **T1566.003 Spearphishing via Service** — outreach over Telegram/LinkedIn posing as investors, recruiters, or journalists; fake-Zoom and Microsoft Teams meeting lures (GhostCall).
- **T1189 Drive-by Compromise** and **T1204.002 User Execution: Malicious File** — fake meeting-software "updates" and trojanized documents/installers.
- **T1195-class supply chain** — malicious Go/npm packages used to reach developers.

### Execution
- **T1059.002 AppleScript** (macOS execution chains), **T1059.007 JavaScript**, **T1059.001 PowerShell / .003 Windows Command Shell** — modular loaders written in Rust, Go, Objective-C, and scripting languages.
- **T1106 Native API**, **T1129 Shared Modules**, **T1657 Financial Theft** (the defining impact-stage objective).

### Persistence & Privilege Escalation
- **T1546.004 Unix Shell Configuration Modification** (novel macOS persistence via shell profiles/zshenv), **T1543 Create/Modify System Process**, **T1547 Boot/Logon Autostart**, **T1548 Abuse Elevation Control** (macOS osascript privilege prompts).

### Defense Evasion
- **T1553.001 Gatekeeper Bypass** and **T1553 Subvert Trust Controls** (signed/notarized or trust-abusing macOS payloads), **T1027 Obfuscated Files**, **T1140 Deobfuscate/Decode**, **T1112 Modify Registry** (Windows), **T1480 Execution Guardrails**.

### Credential Access & Discovery
- **T1555 Credentials from Password Stores** (keychain, browser, wallet data), **T1003 OS Credential Dumping**, **T1082 System Information Discovery**, **T1057 Process Discovery**, **T1518 Software Discovery** (hunting wallet/crypto apps).

### Collection, C2 & Exfiltration
- **T1113 Screen Capture**, **T1056 Input Capture/keylogging**, **T1115 Clipboard Data** (crypto-address swapping), **T1560 Archive Collected Data**.
- **T1071.001 Web Protocols (HTTPS)**, plus WebSockets and the **Telegram Bot API** for exfiltrating screenshots/keystrokes; **T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer**, **T1571 Non-Standard Port**, **T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel**.

### Impact
- **T1657 Financial Theft** (core objective — fraudulent SWIFT transfers, ATM cash-outs, crypto draining), **T1485 Data Destruction** and **T1486 Data Encrypted for Impact** (anti-forensics/cover after bank heists, e.g., disk-wiping post-SWIFT theft), **T1489 Service Stop**.

## Tooling & Malware
- **SnatchCrypto** toolkit — long-running crypto-theft operation against Web3/blockchain firms.
- **RustBucket** — multi-stage macOS backdoor (AppleScript → Swift/Rust stages) targeting crypto staff.
- **KANDYKORN** — macOS implant delivered via fake crypto trading bots to blockchain engineers.
- **ObjCShellz** — Objective-C macOS remote shell used in the RustBucket campaign chain.
- **GhostCall / GhostHire** — 2025 campaigns: fake-Zoom/Teams executive lures (GhostCall) and developer-recruiting lures (GhostHire) delivering modular Rust/Go malware over HTTPS/WebSockets/Telegram.
- **FASTCash** (BeagleBoyz) — ATM cash-out malware injecting fraudulent responses into bank payment switches.
- Windows backdoors shared with Lazarus (e.g., Hoplight/DOWNDELPH-style implants) for SWIFT-environment operations.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2015–2016** — SWIFT bank-heist operations; **Bangladesh Bank** theft (~$81M, 2016) and attempts against other banks.
- **2018** — CISA/FBI **FASTCash** (HIDDEN COBRA) advisories on ATM cash-out attacks; **BeagleBoyz** bank-targeting reporting.
- **2017–2022** — **SnatchCrypto** campaigns against crypto startups and Web3 firms worldwide (Kaspersky).
- **2023** — **RustBucket**, **KANDYKORN**, and **ObjCShellz** macOS campaigns target crypto/blockchain engineers and exchanges.
- **2024** — Continued macOS focus; supply-chain attacks using malicious Go packages; pivot to **Microsoft Teams** impersonation for malware delivery (Microsoft/Sapphire Sleet reporting).
- **Late 2025** — **GhostCall & GhostHire** campaigns (Kaspersky Securelist): fake-Zoom/Teams calls and AI deepfakes target macOS devices of tech/VC executives; 80+ typosquatted C2 domains; HTTPS/WebSocket/Telegram exfiltration.

## Detection & Hunting
- macOS hunting: alert on **osascript/AppleScript** spawning network activity, modifications to shell config files (`.zshenv`, `.zprofile`), and **Gatekeeper bypass** / unsigned binary execution from user directories.
- Detect fake meeting-software: "Zoom/Teams update" prompts, look-alike meeting domains, and binaries masquerading as conferencing clients.
- Monitor for **clipboard-monitoring/crypto-address swapping** and reads of wallet/keychain/browser-credential stores.
- Network: HTTPS to newly-registered typosquatted domains, **WebSocket** C2, and **Telegram Bot API** traffic from endpoints; flag exfil of screenshots/keystrokes.
- In financial environments: monitor SWIFT/payment-switch hosts for anomalous transactions and post-theft wiping (T1485) behavior.
- Watch developer machines for malicious Go/npm packages and "investment/interview" lure files.

## Mitigations
- macOS hardening: enforce Gatekeeper/notarization, restrict osascript, monitor shell-config integrity, deploy macOS-capable EDR.
- Phishing-resistant **FIDO2 MFA**; verify meeting invitations and software updates out-of-band; block conferencing-app spoofing domains.
- Lock down crypto-operations and treasury workflows; hardware wallets and multi-party approval for transfers; out-of-band confirmation.
- Package-management controls (private registries, scanning, pinning) to counter malicious Go/npm packages.
- Network egress filtering; alert on WebSocket/Telegram C2 and typosquatted-domain resolution.
- Targeted awareness for executives, founders, VCs, and blockchain developers on fake-investor and fake-recruiter social engineering.

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only: future intel sweeps add dated entries below -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2025 | Campaign | GhostCall/GhostHire — fake-Zoom/Teams + AI deepfakes targeting macOS execs | Kaspersky Securelist |
| 2023 | Malware | RustBucket/KANDYKORN/ObjCShellz macOS crypto-theft implants | Jamf / Elastic / SentinelOne |
| 2016 | Heist | Bangladesh Bank SWIFT theft (~$81M) | CISA / Mandiant |

## References
- https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0082/
- https://securelist.com/bluenoroff-apt-campaigns-ghostcall-and-ghosthire/117842/
- https://www.picussecurity.com/resource/blog/bluenoroff-group-the-financial-cybercrime-arm-of-lazarus
- https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/threat-assessment-north-korean-threat-groups-2024/

apt.body:
# Charming Kitten (APT35) — APT Profile

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 4 independent sources

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | Iran (assessed IRGC-aligned) |
| Aliases | APT35, Phosphorus, Mint Sandstorm, Magic Hound, TA453, Newscaster, COBALT ILLUSION |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0059 |
| First seen | 2014 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage, influence, surveillance of dissidents |

## Overview
Charming Kitten (APT35 / Phosphorus / Mint Sandstorm) is an Iranian state-sponsored group assessed to operate on behalf of the IRGC. The group is defined by its world-class social engineering: it builds elaborate fake personas (journalists, researchers, conference organizers, NGO staff), conducts extended rapport-building before delivering payloads, and excels at multi-stage credential phishing against high-value individuals. APT35 targets government, academia, journalists, human-rights workers, dissidents, and think-tanks, and has repeatedly targeted political campaigns and current/former officials. It blends low-tech but highly effective phishing (fake login portals, OTP relay) with custom backdoors (POWERSTAR/CharmPower, BellaCiao) and bespoke tools such as HYPERSCRAPE for mass mailbox theft. The group also overlaps with Iranian ransomware/extortion clusters (e.g., COBALT MIRAGE) and influence operations.

## Attribution & Origin
APT35 is attributed to Iran, assessed to act on behalf of the IRGC. Attribution is supported by: targeting of Iranian dissidents, regime critics, and adversary governments; Persian-language artifacts and Iran-aligned working hours; infrastructure and persona overlaps documented across vendor reporting; and US DOJ indictments and sanctions against IRGC-affiliated individuals tied to the activity. Microsoft tracks it as Mint Sandstorm (formerly Phosphorus); CrowdStrike as Charming Kitten; Proofpoint as TA453; Secureworks as COBALT ILLUSION; Mandiant as APT35; iSight/early reporting as Newscaster.

## Motivation & Objectives
- **Espionage** against government, defense, academic, and policy targets relevant to Iranian intelligence.
- **Surveillance and suppression of dissidents**, journalists, and human-rights activists — domestic and diaspora.
- **Influence operations** — fake news personas/sites (Newscaster) to shape narratives and gain access.
- **Opportunistic extortion/ransomware** via related clusters (COBALT MIRAGE) for disruption and funding.

## Target Profile
- **Sectors:** Government, defense, academia, journalism/media, NGOs and human-rights organizations, think-tanks, political campaigns, telecommunications, and energy.
- **Individuals:** Current/former government officials, policy experts on Iran/Middle East, nuclear and defense researchers, and dissidents.
- **Regions:** Strong focus on the United States, Israel, the UK, and Europe; Middle East regional targets.
- **APAC / Australia note:** APT35 routinely targets Iran-focused academics, journalists, and think-tank researchers worldwide, which includes Australian and APAC universities, policy institutes, and diaspora communities. Australian researchers working on Middle East policy, nuclear non-proliferation, or human rights are realistic spearphishing targets via conference/interview-themed lures.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
**Reconnaissance / Resource Development**
- T1598 — Phishing for Information (credential-harvest pretexts)
- T1585 — Establish Accounts (fake personas, sock-puppet email/social accounts)
- T1586 — Compromise Accounts (hijacked legitimate accounts for trust)
- T1583 / T1584 — Acquire / Compromise Infrastructure (lookalike login domains)
- T1588 — Obtain Capabilities

**Initial Access**
- T1566 — Phishing (extended rapport-building spearphishing; fake login portals)
- T1189 — Drive-by Compromise
- T1190 — Exploit Public-Facing Application (e.g., on-prem Exchange/Log4j-era exploitation)

**Execution / Persistence**
- T1059 — Command and Scripting Interpreter (PowerShell — CharmPower/POWERSTAR)
- T1204 — User Execution
- T1547 — Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
- T1136 — Create Account

**Defense Evasion / Credential Access**
- T1027 — Obfuscated Files or Information
- T1564 — Hide Artifacts
- T1003 — OS Credential Dumping
- T1056 — Input Capture
- OTP/2FA relay and real-time phishing of MFA codes (mapped under T1566/T1598 tradecraft)

**Collection / C2 / Exfiltration**
- T1114 — Email Collection (HYPERSCRAPE — bulk Gmail/Yahoo/Outlook theft)
- T1005 — Data from Local System
- T1071 — Application Layer Protocol
- T1573 — Encrypted Channel
- T1105 — Ingress Tool Transfer
- T1567 — Exfiltration Over Web Service (Telegram, cloud services)
- T1486 — Data Encrypted for Impact (related extortion clusters)

## Tooling & Malware
- **POWERSTAR / CharmPower** — modular PowerShell backdoor with C2 fetched from cloud/Telegram and anti-analysis features.
- **HYPERSCRAPE** — custom tool to download and exfiltrate entire victim mailboxes (Gmail, Yahoo, Outlook) using stolen credentials/cookies.
- **BellaCiao** — custom dropper/backdoor family used in more recent intrusions.
- **PupyRAT, DownPaper** — earlier cross-platform RAT and downloader.
- **Phishing kits / fake login portals** with real-time OTP relay; fake conference, interview, and webinar lures.
- **Tickler / HookStick** — additional custom tooling reported in recent campaigns.
- Heavy use of **legitimate cloud and messaging services** (Telegram, Google, Dropbox) for C2 and exfiltration.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2014** — "Newscaster" operation: fake journalist/news-site personas (NewsOnAir) used to social-engineer US/UK/Israeli targets across social media.
- **2017–2018** — Magic Hound / Phosphorus credential-phishing against government, academia, and human-rights targets; PupyRAT/DownPaper.
- **2019** — Targeting of a US presidential campaign and current/former government officials (Microsoft Phosphorus reporting); DOJ/Microsoft domain seizures.
- **2020** — Targeting of medical/COVID research and a security conference (Munich) attendee phishing; "BadBlood" operation against US/Israeli medical professionals.
- **2021** — Exploitation of on-prem Exchange (ProxyShell) and Log4Shell by Phosphorus/COBALT MIRAGE; some intrusions pivot to BitLocker-based extortion.
- **2022 (Aug)** — Google TAG details HYPERSCRAPE for mass mailbox exfiltration.
- **2023** — POWERSTAR/CharmPower campaigns with cloud-hosted, decryption-keyed C2 (Volexity/Check Point reporting).
- **2024** — CharmingCypress/TA453 phishing of Middle East policy experts via fake think-tank/webinar invitations; continued BellaCiao deployment and Tickler activity.

## Detection & Hunting
- **Credential phishing / OTP relay:** Monitor for logins immediately following MFA prompts from anomalous geos/ASNs; alert on new OAuth grants and suspicious session-cookie reuse.
- **Mailbox exfiltration (HYPERSCRAPE):** Detect bulk IMAP/automated mailbox access, mass "mark as unread," and large outbound transfers from webmail.
- **PowerShell backdoors:** Script Block Logging; hunt PowerShell fetching staged payloads/keys from Telegram, Google Docs, or Dropbox.
- **Fake personas/domains:** Track lookalike login domains and newly registered conference/interview-themed domains; brand-monitor for impersonation of staff/journalists.
- **Exchange/Log4j exploitation:** Patch state monitoring and web-shell hunting on internet-facing apps.

## Mitigations
- Deploy phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WebAuthn) to defeat OTP relay; restrict legacy/basic auth.
- User awareness focused on long-con social engineering (journalist/researcher pretexts) for high-risk staff (execs, researchers, comms).
- Lock down OAuth app consent; monitor and revoke risky grants; enforce session-token protection and continuous access evaluation.
- Patch internet-facing services promptly (Exchange, VPNs, Java/Log4j components).
- Provide hardened account protection / Advanced Protection enrollment for high-risk individuals (officials, dissidents, researchers).

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |

## References

- https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/actor/apt35
- https://blog.google/threat-analysis-group/new-iranian-apt-data-extraction-tool/
- https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2023/04/18/nation-state-threat-actor-mint-sandstorm-refines-tradecraft-to-attack-high-value-targets/
- https://www.volexity.com/blog/2023/06/28/charming-kitten-updates-powerstar-with-an-interplanetary-twist/

apt.body:
# FIN7 — APT Profile

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 4 independent sources

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | Financially-motivated cybercrime (Eastern European nexus; arrests in multiple countries) |
| Aliases | Carbon Spider, GOLD NIAGARA, ITG14, ELBRUS, Sangria Tempest, Coreid |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0046 |
| First seen | 2013 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Financial — payment-card theft, then big-game-hunting ransomware/extortion |

## Overview
FIN7 is one of the most prolific and adaptable financially-motivated cybercrime groups, active since at least 2013. It began as a payment-card (carding) operation, breaching point-of-sale (POS) environments across retail, restaurant, and hospitality chains and stealing tens of millions of card records — causing well over a billion dollars in losses. The group is renowned for operational sophistication rare among criminal actors: bespoke malware (Carbanak, GRIFFON, Lizar/Tirion), polished social-engineering supported by a front company ("Combi Security") that recruited unwitting penetration testers, and continual tooling innovation (e.g., BadUSB mailings, EDR-killer tools). Since ~2020, FIN7 pivoted to big-game-hunting ransomware, operating and affiliating with multiple RaaS programs (DarkSide, BlackMatter, ALPHV/BlackCat, Cl0p, REvil). FIN7 shares the Carbanak malware lineage with the original Carbanak/Anunak group but is tracked as a distinct organization.

## Attribution & Origin
FIN7 is a financially-motivated criminal enterprise (not state-sponsored), assessed to have an Eastern European core. Attribution and disruption are anchored by US DOJ action: multiple high-ranking members were arrested (2018) and convicted, with the "Combi Security" front company exposed as a recruiting shell. FIN7 shares tooling and tradecraft with the **Carbanak Group (Anunak)** — both use the Carbanak backdoor — but MITRE and most vendors track them separately, as the Carbanak malware was used by at least two distinct groups. Vendor naming: Carbon Spider (CrowdStrike), GOLD NIAGARA (Secureworks), ELBRUS / Sangria Tempest (Microsoft), ITG14 (IBM), Coreid (Symantec).

## Motivation & Objectives
- **Payment-card theft** — historic core: POS malware to scrape and exfiltrate magstripe/track data for carding-market resale.
- **Big-game-hunting ransomware & extortion** — since ~2020, encryption + data-theft extortion against larger enterprises for multi-million-dollar ransoms.
- **Access brokering / RaaS operation** — building and selling access, running/affiliating with ransomware brands.
- **Tooling commercialization** — selling EDR-evasion tools (e.g., AvNeutralizer/AuKill) to other criminal groups.

## Target Profile
- **Sectors:** Retail, restaurant, hospitality, financial services, software, consulting, medical equipment, cloud services, media, transportation, pharmaceutical, and utilities — broadly opportunistic but historically POS-heavy verticals.
- **Regions:** Predominantly the United States; significant activity across Europe, the UK, and globally.
- **APAC / Australia note:** As FIN7 shifted to ransomware big-game hunting, its targeting broadened globally and opportunistically. Australian and APAC enterprises — particularly retail/hospitality chains with POS estates and mid-to-large organizations exposed via internet-facing services or phishing — are within scope, especially given FIN7's affiliation with RaaS programs that operate worldwide.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
**Resource Development**
- T1583 — Acquire Infrastructure
- T1587 — Develop Capabilities (custom malware/loaders)
- T1588 — Obtain Capabilities
- T1195 — Supply Chain Compromise (trojanized tools/installers)

**Initial Access**
- T1566 — Phishing (themed lures; malicious docs; later BadUSB devices mailed to targets)
- T1190 — Exploit Public-Facing Application
- T1133 — External Remote Services
- T1078 — Valid Accounts

**Execution**
- T1059 — Command and Scripting Interpreter (PowerShell, JavaScript, VBScript)
- T1204 — User Execution
- T1559 — Inter-Process Communication
- T1053 — Scheduled Task/Job

**Persistence / Privilege Escalation**
- T1547 — Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
- T1543 — Create or Modify System Process (services)
- T1546 — Event Triggered Execution
- T1136 — Create Account

**Defense Evasion**
- T1218 — System Binary Proxy Execution (mshta, rundll32, etc.)
- T1027 — Obfuscated Files or Information
- T1140 — Deobfuscate/Decode Files
- T1562 — Impair Defenses (EDR killers — AuKill/AvNeutralizer)
- T1553 — Subvert Trust Controls (code-signing abuse)
- T1564 — Hide Artifacts

**Credential Access / Lateral Movement**
- T1003 — OS Credential Dumping (Mimikatz)
- T1021 — Remote Services (RDP, SMB; Cobalt Strike lateral movement)

**Command and Control**
- T1071 — Application Layer Protocol
- T1105 — Ingress Tool Transfer
- T1572 — Protocol Tunneling
- T1219 — Remote Access Software (NetSupport RAT, legitimate RMM abuse)

**Impact**
- T1486 — Data Encrypted for Impact (ransomware deployment)
- T1657 — Financial Theft (carding / extortion)

## Tooling & Malware
- **Carbanak** — flagship backdoor (shared lineage with the Carbanak/Anunak group); espionage-grade RAT for finance-network intrusion.
- **GRIFFON / BABYMETAL** — JavaScript-based reconnaissance/staging implant.
- **POWERTRASH / DICELOADER (Lizar/Tirion)** — in-memory PowerShell shellcode loader and modular post-exploitation framework.
- **PowerPlant** — PowerShell-based backdoor.
- **POS scrapers** — memory-scraping malware for payment-card track data (early era).
- **NetSupport RAT / Anchor / Cobalt Strike** — RATs and post-exploitation/C2.
- **AuKill / AvNeutralizer** — bring-your-own-vulnerable-driver EDR-killer tooling (also sold to other actors).
- **BadUSB ("BadBox") mailings** — weaponized USB drives mailed to targets impersonating Amazon/HHS.
- **Front company "Combi Security"** for recruiting and operational cover.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2013–2015** — Carbanak-era intrusions into banks and financial institutions (shared tooling era).
- **2015–2017** — Mass POS/carding campaigns against US retail, restaurant, and hospitality chains; phishing with weaponized Office docs and the "Combi Security" front.
- **2017** — Sophisticated phishing using booking/complaint lures; heavy use of shim databases and LNK persistence.
- **2018 (Aug)** — US DOJ announces arrests of three high-ranking FIN7 members; "Combi Security" front exposed.
- **2020** — Pivot to big-game-hunting ransomware; FIN7 (Carbon Spider) linked to DarkSide RaaS launch.
- **2021** — Operation of/affiliation with BlackMatter; FBI warns of BadUSB packages mailed to organizations to deliver ransomware.
- **2022** — Mandiant consolidates multiple UNC clusters into FIN7; links to ALPHV/BlackCat, Cl0p, REvil affiliations; ongoing tooling evolution (Lizar/Tirion).
- **2023** — Microsoft (Sangria Tempest) and others tie FIN7 to Cl0p ransomware precursor activity and continued RMM/EDR-evasion tradecraft.
- **2024** — Reporting on FIN7 developing and selling AvNeutralizer/AuKill EDR-killer tooling and spinning up fake/AI-themed websites to distribute malware.

## Detection & Hunting
- **Phishing & proxy execution:** Hunt Office docs spawning mshta/rundll32/wscript/powershell; alert on LNK and HTA execution chains.
- **EDR tampering:** Detect driver-load events from known vulnerable drivers (BYOVD) and abrupt sensor/service stops (AuKill/AvNeutralizer behavior).
- **PowerShell loaders:** Script Block Logging; hunt in-memory shellcode loaders (POWERTRASH) and Cobalt Strike beacon patterns.
- **RMM abuse:** Inventory and alert on unsanctioned remote-access tools (NetSupport, AnyDesk, ScreenConnect).
- **POS environments:** Monitor for memory-scraping process behavior and unexpected outbound transfers from POS/segmented networks.
- **Ransomware pre-cursors:** Watch for mass file access, shadow-copy deletion, and lateral RDP/SMB bursts.

## Mitigations
- Deploy phishing-resistant MFA and harden email security (attachment sandboxing, block HTA/macros from internet).
- Application allowlisting and constrained-language PowerShell; block/abuse-monitor LOLBins (mshta, rundll32).
- Enable tamper protection on EDR and vulnerable-driver blocklists (BYOVD mitigation).
- Network segmentation of POS/payment environments with PCI-aligned controls and egress filtering.
- Offline, tested backups and rapid-response ransomware playbooks; restrict/inventory RMM tooling.
- Train mailroom/staff to treat unsolicited USB devices as hostile (BadUSB mailings).

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |

## References
- https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0046/
- https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/actor/fin7
- https://www.mandiant.com/resources/blog/evolution-of-fin7
- https://www.justice.gov/opa/pr/three-members-notorious-international-cybercrime-group-fin7-custody-role-attacking-over-100
- https://www.picussecurity.com/resource/fin7-cybercrime-group-evolution-from-pos-attacks-to-ransomware-as-a-service-raas-operations

apt.body:
# Gamaredon — APT Profile

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 5 independent sources

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | Russia — FSB 18th Center, operating from occupied Crimea |
| Aliases | Primitive Bear, Shuckworm, ACTINIUM, Armageddon, Aqua Blizzard, UAC-0010 |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0047 |
| First seen | 2013 |
| Status | Active (highest-tempo Russian actor against Ukraine) |
| Motivation | Espionage, pre-positioning for sabotage, influence |

## Overview
Gamaredon is a Russian state-sponsored cyber-espionage group attributed by the Security Service of Ukraine (SSU) to the FSB. It has operated continuously since at least 2013, surging dramatically after Russia's annexation of Crimea and again after the February 2022 full-scale invasion. Gamaredon is notable not for technical sophistication but for sheer operational tempo, persistence, and volume — it runs near-continuous spear-phishing campaigns, rotates infrastructure aggressively (often via fast-flux and free dynamic-DNS providers), and re-compromises targets repeatedly. The group is effectively the FSB's persistent-access workhorse against Ukraine, harvesting documents and credentials and maintaining footholds that can be leveraged by other, more capable Russian units.

## Attribution & Origin
In November 2021 the SSU publicly named five FSB officers (based in occupied Crimea, formerly Ukrainian SBU personnel who defected after 2014) as the operators behind Gamaredon. The group is assessed to sit within the FSB's 18th Center (Information Security Center) and operates out of Sevastopol. Attribution confidence is unusually high for an APT due to Ukrainian counterintelligence access, leaked operator details, and consistent Russian-language and Crimea-based infrastructure artifacts.

## Motivation & Objectives
- Strategic and tactical intelligence collection against Ukrainian government, military, and security services.
- Document and credential theft to feed Russian decision-making and battlefield awareness.
- Pre-positioning persistent access that can be handed to other FSB/GRU units for follow-on espionage or destructive operations.
- Demoralization/influence: occasional defacement and disruptive actions aligned with wartime psychological operations.

## Target Profile
Gamaredon's targeting is overwhelmingly Ukraine-centric: government ministries, the armed forces, the SSU/SBU, law enforcement, defense industry, energy sector, NGOs, and journalists. Since 2022 it has also been observed targeting NATO-member entities and organizations supporting Ukraine. **APAC relevance:** while Ukraine remains the priority, allied intelligence services and security vendors track Gamaredon globally because its phishing toolkits and GammaSteel/Pterodo families are widely circulated IOCs; APAC and Australian defense, government, and think-tank organizations supporting Ukraine policy are plausible secondary collection targets and should treat Gamaredon TTPs as in-scope for their threat models.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK

### Initial Access
- **T1566.001 Spearphishing Attachment** / **T1566.002 Spearphishing Link** — Ukrainian-language lure documents (military orders, court notices, government memos) delivered by email and messaging apps.
- **T1221 Template Injection** — weaponized Office documents pull a remote template that fetches the next stage, evading static AV.

### Execution
- **T1059.001 PowerShell** and **T1059.005 Visual Basic** — VBScript/VBA macros and PowerShell loaders (GammaLoad, PowerPunch) drive the kill chain.
- **T1204.002 User Execution: Malicious File** — relies on the victim enabling content / opening the lure.
- **T1106 Native API** and **T1559.001 COM** — GamaCOM and related tools abuse COM for execution and lateral movement.

### Persistence
- **T1547.001 Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder** and **T1053.005 Scheduled Task** — primary persistence.
- **T1137.001 Office Template Macros** — hijacks Normal.dotm so every Word launch reloads the implant.

### Defense Evasion
- **T1027 Obfuscated/Encoded Files**, **T1140 Deobfuscate/Decode**, **T1112 Modify Registry**, **T1218 System Binary Proxy Execution**, **T1564 Hide Artifacts**, **T1497 Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion**.

### Credential Access / Discovery
- **T1552 Unsecured Credentials**, **T1083 File/Directory Discovery**, **T1057 Process Discovery**, **T1518 Software Discovery**, **T1082 System Information Discovery**.

### Collection & Exfiltration
- **T1005 Data from Local System**, **T1039 Data from Network Shared Drive**, **T1025 Data from Removable Media**, **T1113 Screen Capture**, **T1056.001 Keylogging**, **T1119 Automated Collection**.
- **T1091 Replication Through Removable Media** — USB-borne propagation (notably used to bridge air-gapped/segmented Ukrainian military networks).
- **T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel**.

### Command & Control
- **T1071.001 Web Protocols (HTTP/S)**, **T1102.002 Bidirectional Communication** (uses Telegram and Telegraph for dead-drop C2 resolution), **T1568 Dynamic Resolution / fast-flux**, **T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer**, **T1571 Non-Standard Port**.

### Infrastructure
- **T1583 Acquire Infrastructure**, **T1587 Develop Capabilities**, **T1588 Obtain Capabilities**, **T1608 Stage Capabilities** — heavy use of throwaway domains and dynamic-DNS.

## Tooling & Malware
- **Pterodo / Pteranodon (S0147)** — flagship modular backdoor; persistence, file theft, payload download.
- **GammaLoad / GammaDrop** — VBScript downloader and dropper chain.
- **GammaSteel** — PowerShell information stealer; 2024–2025 variants exfiltrate documents and harvest data from USB drives, with added obfuscation and PowerShell-based reconnaissance.
- **PowerPunch / GamaCOM / LakeFlash / QuietCanary / PteroPSDoor / PteroGraphin** — loaders, COM-based tools, and newer implants (some using Telegram/Telegraph and even Cloudflare tunnels for resilient C2).

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2014** — Activity surges after the annexation of Crimea; group pivots to all-Ukraine targeting.
- **2017** — Cisco Talos and others document the "Pteranodon" family and Gamaredon's distinctive infrastructure footprints.
- **Nov 2021** — SSU publicly attributes Gamaredon to five named FSB officers in occupied Crimea.
- **2022 (full-scale invasion)** — Operational tempo spikes; Microsoft (ACTINIUM/Aqua Blizzard) and CERT-UA report relentless phishing against Ukrainian government and military.
- **2023** — Symantec (Shuckworm) documents USB-propagating tools used to reach Ukrainian military networks; long-dwell intrusions observed.
- **2024–2025** — Updated **GammaSteel** campaigns reported (e.g., Symantec/CSO Online), featuring improved obfuscation, USB data theft, PowerShell reconnaissance, and use of legitimate web services (Telegram, Telegraph, Cloudflare) for stealthy C2.

## Detection & Hunting
- Hunt for **remote template injection**: Office docs reaching out to external URLs on open; monitor `WINWORD.EXE` spawning `mshta.exe`, `powershell.exe`, `wscript.exe`, or `cmd.exe`.
- Alert on **Normal.dotm** modifications and new macros in the Office Startup/Templates folders.
- Detect **scheduled tasks** and **Run-key** entries created by script interpreters.
- Monitor outbound connections to dynamic-DNS providers, Telegram/Telegraph API endpoints, and newly-registered domains.
- Flag **VBScript/PowerShell** with heavy obfuscation, base64, and string concatenation; look for GammaLoad/GammaSteel string and behavioral indicators (CERT-UA publishes regular IOC bundles for UAC-0010).
- USB hunting: watch for autorun-style LNK/EXE drops and copies of implants to removable media (T1091).

## Mitigations
- Disable Office macros from the internet (Mark-of-the-Web enforcement); block remote template loading via Group Policy.
- Application control / WDAC and constrained-language-mode PowerShell; enable PowerShell script-block and module logging.
- Email filtering with sandboxing for Office/HTA/script attachments; warn on external-template fetches.
- Restrict and monitor removable-media use on sensitive/military networks.
- Network egress filtering and DNS monitoring; block known dynamic-DNS and abused-service C2 where feasible.
- Subscribe to CERT-UA UAC-0010 advisories and operationalize their IOC feeds.

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only: future intel sweeps add dated entries below -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2025 | Malware update | GammaSteel refreshed with USB theft + improved obfuscation | Symantec / CSO Online |
| 2021-11 | Attribution | SSU names five FSB officers behind Gamaredon | SSU Ukraine |

## References
- https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0047/
- https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0147/
- https://www.csoonline.com/article/3959665/russian-shuckworm-apt-is-back-with-updated-gammasteel-malware.html
- https://socradar.io/blog/dark-web-profile-gamaredon-apt/
- https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/actor/gamaredon_group
- https://blogs.cisco.com/security/network-footprints-of-gamaredon-group

apt.body:
# Kimsuky — APT Profile

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 6 independent sources

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | North Korea — Reconnaissance General Bureau (RGB) |
| Aliases | Velvet Chollima, Black Banshee, Thallium, Emerald Sleet, APT43, TA427 |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0094 |
| First seen | 2012 (publicly documented 2013) |
| Status | Active (one of DPRK's highest-volume espionage actors) |
| Motivation | Espionage; influence; opportunistic financial (crypto) |

## Overview
Kimsuky is a North Korean state-sponsored cyber-espionage group operating since at least 2012. Attributed to the RGB, its core mission is strategic intelligence collection — Korean Peninsula geopolitics, foreign policy, sanctions, nuclear/military matters, and the activities of experts who shape policy toward North Korea. Kimsuky is distinguished by patient, research-heavy spear-phishing: operators build rapport with targets over multiple benign emails (a hallmark of the TA427/APT43 cluster) before delivering malware or credential-harvesting links. CISA, the FBI, NSA, and South Korea's NIS have issued joint advisories on the group, including warnings about its abuse of weak DMARC policies to spoof trusted senders.

## Attribution & Origin
Kimsuky is assessed to operate under the DPRK's Reconnaissance General Bureau. Mandiant tracks the broader cluster as APT43 and assesses it conducts espionage in direct support of RGB intelligence requirements while sometimes self-funding operations through cybercrime. Proofpoint tracks the email-centric subset as TA427. The U.S. and ROK governments have publicly attributed Kimsuky activity to North Korea in multiple joint advisories (2020, 2023, 2024).

## Motivation & Objectives
- **Espionage (primary):** collect foreign-policy, national-security, sanctions, and nuclear-related intelligence to inform DPRK leadership.
- **Influence/access:** compromise journalists, academics, and policy experts to monitor and shape narratives and gain access to their networks.
- **Financial (secondary/opportunistic):** occasional cryptocurrency theft and use of crime to self-fund espionage infrastructure.

## Target Profile
Primary targets are South Korean government, military, and the foreign-policy/think-tank community; the United States; Japan; and Europe. Kimsuky heavily targets individuals: North Korea experts, academics, journalists, defectors, and NGO staff. **APAC / Australia relevance is high** — beyond South Korea and Japan, Kimsuky targets the wider Indo-Pacific policy ecosystem. Australian think tanks, universities, and government bodies engaged in North Korea, China, and regional security policy are credible targets; the group's spoofed-academic-portal and "expert outreach" lures map directly onto Australian and APAC research institutions. Treat unsolicited "interview/collaboration request" emails to regional Korea/security experts as a Kimsuky indicator.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK

### Reconnaissance
- **T1589 Gather Victim Identity Info**, **T1591 Gather Victim Org Info**, **T1598.003 Phishing for Information: Spearphishing Link** — extensive pre-attack profiling and benign "rapport-building" emails.
- **T1585 Establish Accounts / T1586 Compromise Accounts** — fake personas (journalists, academics) and compromised legitimate mailboxes.

### Initial Access
- **T1566.001 Spearphishing Attachment** / **T1566.002 Spearphishing Link** — malicious HWP/Office docs, LNK files, and credential-harvesting pages mimicking webmail and university/government portals.
- **T1190 Exploit Public-Facing Application** and **T1133 External Remote Services** — opportunistic exploitation.

### Execution
- **T1059.001 PowerShell**, **T1059.003 Windows Command Shell**, **T1059.005 Visual Basic** — BabyShark and PowerShell-based loaders/keyloggers (e.g., MECHANICAL).
- **T1204 User Execution**, **T1218 System Binary Proxy Execution** (mshta, regsvr32), **T1559 Inter-Process Communication**.

### Persistence
- **T1547.001 Registry Run Keys / Startup**, **T1053.005 Scheduled Task**, **T1543 Create/Modify System Process**, **T1546 Event-Triggered Execution**, **T1505.003 Web Shell**.
- **T1176 Browser Extensions** — malicious Chrome extensions (e.g., "Sharpext"/GoldDragon-era) to steal webmail content from logged-in sessions.

### Defense Evasion
- **T1027 Obfuscated Files**, **T1140 Deobfuscate/Decode**, **T1112 Modify Registry**, **T1564 Hide Artifacts**, **T1656 Impersonation** (impersonating trusted contacts/brands), **T1553 Subvert Trust Controls**.

### Credential Access
- **T1056.001 Keylogging** (MECHANICAL PowerShell keylogger), **T1555 Credentials from Password Stores**, **T1003 OS Credential Dumping**, **T1111 Multi-Factor Authentication Interception**, **T1539 Steal Web Session Cookie**, **T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle** (phishing proxies).

### Collection & Exfiltration
- **T1114.002 Remote Email Collection**, **T1113 Screen Capture**, **T1005 Data from Local System**, **T1560 Archive Collected Data**.
- **T1567 Exfiltration Over Web Service** (cloud/webmail), **T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel**, **T1048 Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol**.

### Command & Control
- **T1071.001 Web Protocols**, **T1102 Web Service** (legitimate cloud services for C2), **T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer**, **T1573 Encrypted Channel**, **T1219 Remote Access Software** (commodity RATs like Quasar/TeamViewer abuse).

## Tooling & Malware
- **BabyShark** — PowerShell/VBScript first-stage recon and C2 implant; a long-standing Kimsuky staple.
- **AppleSeed** — modular Windows backdoor (and an Android variant) for persistent access, keylogging, screenshotting, and file theft.
- **ReconShark / RandomQuery** — reconnaissance downloaders used to profile victims before deploying heavier payloads.
- **GoldDragon / Gold Dragon, FlowerPower, KGH_SPY** — backdoors and stealers from earlier campaigns.
- **MECHANICAL** — PowerShell keylogger; pairs with NirSoft tools (e.g., SniffPass) for credential harvesting.
- **Quasar RAT and other commodity RATs** — used alongside custom tooling.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2013–2014** — Early "Kimsuky" activity documented (Kaspersky) targeting South Korean think tanks.
- **2018–2019** — BabyShark campaigns against U.S. national-security think tanks and Korea experts.
- **2020** — CISA/FBI alert AA20-301A details Kimsuky TTPs and tooling.
- **2022** — "Sharpext" malicious browser extension steals Gmail/AOL content from active sessions (Volexity).
- **Early 2024** — **DEEP#GOSU** multi-stage PowerShell campaign against Windows systems (Securonix); university-portal credential-phishing campaigns documented.
- **2024** — NSA/FBI/State Dept. and ROK advisory warns of Kimsuky abusing weak/misconfigured **DMARC** to spoof trusted senders for spearphishing.
- **2024–2025** — Continued TA427/APT43 "expert engagement" phishing against U.S., ROK, Japanese, and European policy targets; ongoing AppleSeed and ReconShark use.

## Detection & Hunting
- Hunt for **rapport-building email patterns**: repeated benign messages from external personas to policy/academic staff, followed by an attachment or link.
- Detect **HWP/LNK/Office** files spawning `mshta.exe`, `powershell.exe`, `regsvr32.exe`, or `cmd.exe`.
- Monitor for **malicious browser extensions** and unexpected reads of webmail session data.
- Alert on **PowerShell keylogging** behaviors and NirSoft tool execution (SniffPass, etc.).
- Watch for **MFA/cookie theft** and adversary-in-the-middle phishing infrastructure (newly-registered look-alike webmail/portal domains).
- Validate inbound mail authentication; alert on **DMARC/SPF/DKIM** failures and spoofed trusted senders.

## Mitigations
- Enforce **DMARC reject (p=reject)**, SPF, and DKIM; alert on authentication failures.
- Phishing-resistant **FIDO2/WebAuthn MFA** to defeat cookie/OTP theft and AiTM.
- Macro and script hardening (block internet macros, constrained-language PowerShell, script-block logging).
- Restrict and inventory **browser extensions** via enterprise policy.
- User awareness for high-risk roles (Korea/security experts, journalists, academics): treat unsolicited collaboration/interview requests with suspicion.
- Operationalize CISA/NSA/NIS Kimsuky IOCs and monitor for AppleSeed/BabyShark/ReconShark indicators.

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only: future intel sweeps add dated entries below -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2024 | Advisory | NSA/FBI/ROK warn of Kimsuky DMARC abuse for spoofed spearphishing | NSA / FBI / State Dept. |
| 2024 | Campaign | DEEP#GOSU multi-stage PowerShell attack on Windows | Securonix |

## References
- https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0094/
- https://www.huntress.com/threat-library/threat-actors/kimsuky
- https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/actor/kimsuky
- https://www.picussecurity.com/resource/blog/exposing-the-steps-of-the-kimsuky-apt-group
- https://www.globalsecurity.org/intell/ops/apt43.htm
- https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa20-301a

apt.body:
# Lazarus Group — APT Profile

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 6 independent sources

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | North Korea — Reconnaissance General Bureau (RGB) |
| Aliases | Hidden Cobra, Labyrinth Chollima, ZINC, Diamond Sleet, APT38, TraderTraitor |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0032 |
| First seen | 2009 (Operation Troy) |
| Status | Active (most prolific DPRK financial + espionage actor) |
| Motivation | Financial (regime revenue), espionage, sabotage/destruction |

## Overview
Lazarus Group is North Korea's flagship state-sponsored threat actor, active since at least 2009 and attributed to the RGB. It is an umbrella organization spanning multiple subgroups — including the financially focused **APT38/BlueNoroff** and **TraderTraitor** crypto-theft cells, and **Andariel** for espionage/ransomware. Lazarus is responsible for some of the most consequential cyber events in history: the destructive 2014 Sony Pictures hack, the 2016 Bangladesh Bank SWIFT heist, the 2017 WannaCry ransomware outbreak, and a string of record-breaking cryptocurrency thefts. The group is unusual among nation-state actors in that a primary objective is generating hard currency to fund the DPRK regime and circumvent international sanctions.

## Attribution & Origin
The U.S. DOJ has indicted named North Korean RGB operators (notably Park Jin Hyok in 2018) for Lazarus operations. CISA/FBI "HIDDEN COBRA" advisories, Treasury (OFAC) sanctions, and vendor reporting from Mandiant, Kaspersky, Microsoft (Diamond Sleet/ZINC), and others consistently attribute Lazarus to North Korea. The FBI and partners have publicly attributed major crypto heists (Ronin, Harmony, DMM Bitcoin, Bybit) to Lazarus/TraderTraitor.

## Motivation & Objectives
- **Financial (dominant):** theft from banks (SWIFT), cryptocurrency exchanges, DeFi bridges, and individuals to fund the regime and weapons programs; estimated billions stolen.
- **Espionage:** defense, aerospace, energy, and technology intelligence (overlap with Andariel/APT37 collection priorities).
- **Sabotage/destruction:** wiper and ransomware operations (Sony 2014, WannaCry 2017) for coercion and disruption.

## Target Profile
Lazarus targets globally across cryptocurrency and financial services, defense/aerospace, energy, technology, media, and supply-chain vendors. It runs persistent fake-recruiter ("Operation Dream Job") and ClickFix campaigns against engineers and crypto/blockchain staff. **APAC / Australia & financial/crypto theft relevance is high:** Australian and APAC cryptocurrency exchanges, blockchain startups, fintechs, and their employees are squarely in scope. Lazarus has repeatedly hit exchanges across the Asia-Pacific (e.g., DMM Bitcoin in Japan, and numerous regional platforms), and Australian crypto firms and developers face the same fake-job, supply-chain (malicious npm/PyPI), and social-engineering vectors. Treat unsolicited recruiter outreach, "coding test" repos, and fake Zoom/wallet tooling as high-risk Lazarus indicators in this sector.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK

### Initial Access
- **T1566.001/.003 Spearphishing (Attachment / via Service)** — "Dream Job" fake recruiter lures via LinkedIn/Telegram with trojanized documents and "coding challenges."
- **T1189 Drive-by Compromise** — watering holes and a Feb 2025 Chrome zero-day delivered via a fake gaming site (AI-generated content for credibility).
- **T1195 Supply Chain Compromise** — malicious npm/PyPI packages (BeaverTail/InvisibleFerret) and the 3CX/X_TRADER software supply-chain cascade.

### Execution
- **T1204.002 User Execution: Malicious File**, **T1059.001 PowerShell / T1059.005 Visual Basic / T1059.007 JavaScript**, **T1106 Native API**, **T1203 Exploitation for Client Execution**.

### Persistence & Privilege Escalation
- **T1543.003 Windows Service**, **T1547.001 Registry Run Keys/Startup**, **T1053 Scheduled Task/Job**, **T1574 Hijack Execution Flow** (DLL side-loading).
- **T1068-class driver exploitation** — exploited **CVE-2024-38193** (Windows AFD.sys BYOVD/zero-day) for SYSTEM-level privilege escalation (FudModule rootkit chain). [Privilege Escalation]

### Defense Evasion
- **T1027 Obfuscated/Encoded Files**, **T1140 Deobfuscate/Decode**, **T1202 Indirect Command Execution**, **T1218 System Binary Proxy Execution**, **T1220 XSL Script Processing**, **T1221 Template Injection**, **T1553 Subvert Trust Controls** (stolen/abused code-signing certs), **T1564 Hide Artifacts**.

### Credential Access & Discovery
- **T1110 Brute Force**, **T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle**, **T1087 Account Discovery**, **T1083 File/Directory Discovery**.

### Collection, C2 & Exfiltration
- **T1056 Input Capture**, **T1560 Archive Collected Data**, **T1005 Data from Local System**.
- **T1071.001 Web Protocols**, **T1573 Encrypted Channel**, **T1008 Fallback Channels**, **T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer**, **T1001 Data Obfuscation**.
- **T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel**, **T1567.002 Exfiltration to Cloud Storage**.

### Impact
- **T1485 Data Destruction** and **T1491 Defacement** (Sony 2014, Guardians of Peace), **T1486 Data Encrypted for Impact** (WannaCry). [Impact]

## Tooling & Malware
- **Manuscrypt / NukeSped / Comebacker** — modular backdoors used across campaigns.
- **AppleJeus** — trojanized cryptocurrency trading apps (Windows & macOS) for crypto theft.
- **BLINDINGCAN, FALLCHILL, Volgmer, DTrack** — RATs/backdoors from HIDDEN COBRA advisories.
- **BeaverTail / InvisibleFerret** — JavaScript/Python stealers in the "Contagious Interview" fake-job + supply-chain campaigns.
- **WannaCry** — self-propagating ransomware (EternalBlue) behind the 2017 global outbreak.
- **FudModule rootkit** — kernel-level AV/EDR tampering delivered via BYOVD.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2009 / 2013** — Operation Troy and DarkSeoul wiper attacks against South Korea.
- **Nov 2014** — Destructive **Sony Pictures** hack (Guardians of Peace) — wiper + data leak.
- **Feb 2016** — **Bangladesh Bank SWIFT heist** (~$81M stolen).
- **May 2017** — **WannaCry** global ransomware outbreak.
- **2018** — DOJ indicts Park Jin Hyok; AppleJeus crypto-theft campaigns surface.
- **Mar 2022** — **Ronin Network bridge** hack (~$620M, Axie Infinity) attributed to Lazarus.
- **2023** — **JumpCloud** cloud identity provider breach (TraderTraitor); 3CX/X_TRADER supply-chain cascade.
- **May 2024** — **DMM Bitcoin** (Japan) heist (~$308M) attributed to TraderTraitor by FBI/NPA.
- **Aug 2024** — Exploitation of **CVE-2024-38193** (AFD.sys zero-day) for SYSTEM access.
- **Feb 2025** — **Bybit** exchange heist (~$1.5B in Ethereum) — largest crypto theft on record; Chrome zero-day delivered via fake gaming site.
- **2024–2025** — Adoption of **ClickFix** social engineering in fake job-interview crypto-theft chains; AI-generated personas/recruiters on LinkedIn.

## Detection & Hunting
- Hunt for **fake-recruiter** activity: trojanized "job description"/"coding test" files, ISO/LNK lures, and developer machines pulling unusual npm/PyPI packages (BeaverTail/InvisibleFerret IOCs).
- Detect **DLL side-loading** and execution from unusual paths; alert on signed binaries loading unsigned/abnormal DLLs.
- Monitor for **BYOVD** — vulnerable-driver loads (e.g., AFD.sys exploit patterns) and kernel-tampering behaviors consistent with FudModule.
- Watch crypto/finance endpoints for **trojanized trading apps** (AppleJeus) and fake wallet/Zoom installers.
- Network: encrypted C2 with fallback channels, exfil to cloud storage, connections to newly-registered look-alike crypto/recruiting domains.
- For exchanges: monitor privileged-operator workstations and multisig signing infrastructure for AiTM and UI-spoofing (Bybit-style) tradecraft.

## Mitigations
- Phishing-resistant **FIDO2 MFA**; strict controls on developer and crypto-operations endpoints.
- **Application control / WDAC**, vulnerable-driver blocklists (Microsoft recommended driver block rules) to counter BYOVD.
- Lock down package management (private registries, dependency pinning, scanning) to counter malicious npm/PyPI.
- Segregate and monitor signing/multisig and treasury operations; enforce out-of-band transaction verification.
- Patch promptly (Chrome, Windows kernel/AFD.sys, 3CX-type supply-chain risks); EDR with kernel-tamper protection.
- Security awareness for engineers/crypto staff on fake-recruiter, "coding test," and ClickFix lures.

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only: future intel sweeps add dated entries below -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2025-02 | Heist | Bybit ~$1.5B Ethereum theft — largest crypto heist on record | FBI / Picus |
| 2024-05 | Heist | DMM Bitcoin ~$308M theft attributed to TraderTraitor | FBI / NPA Japan |
| 2024-08 | Vulnerability | CVE-2024-38193 AFD.sys zero-day used for SYSTEM access | Gen/Avast |
| 2026-06 | Malware | Suspected BeaverTail sample `vscode-bootstrap.sh` → code-beautify[.]com (2 VT) — developer lure | @malwrhunterteam/@LOPsec (X) |

## References
- https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0032/
- https://www.picussecurity.com/resource/blog/fbi-north-korean-lazarus-group-bybit-crypto-heist
- https://www.wiz.io/blog/north-korean-tradertraitor-crypto-heist
- https://www.huntress.com/threat-library/threat-actors/lazarus-group
- https://www.infoblox.com/blog/security/lazarus-group-targets-financial-services-and-cryptocurrency-sector/
- https://www.picussecurity.com/resource/blog/lazarus-group-apt38-explained-timeline-ttps-and-major-attacks

apt.body:
# Mustang Panda — APT Profile

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 7 independent sources

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | China (MSS-aligned cyber-espionage) |
| Aliases | TA416, RedDelta, BRONZE PRESIDENT, Stately Taurus, Earth Preta, Camaro Dragon, TWILL TYPHOON, UNC6384, LUMINOUS MOTH |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0129 |
| First seen | 2012 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage / intelligence collection |

## Overview
Mustang Panda is one of the most prolific China-nexus cyber-espionage groups, operating since at least 2012 and tracked by MITRE ATT&CK as **G0129**. The group is best known for high-volume spear-phishing campaigns delivering the PlugX remote access trojan and, more recently, a refreshed arsenal (ToneShell, Hodur, PUBLOAD, StarProxy). It conducts persistent collection against governments, foreign-affairs ministries, NGOs, think tanks, and religious/ethnic-minority organisations whose activities are of interest to Beijing. The group is notable for its rapid retooling, USB-worm propagation, and heavy use of DLL side-loading with signed, legitimate binaries.

## Attribution & Origin
Mustang Panda is assessed with high confidence as a China-based, state-aligned actor whose targeting consistently maps to PRC strategic and intelligence priorities (Taiwan, Tibetan/Uyghur diaspora, ASEAN diplomacy, Belt-and-Road partner states). Vendors variously cluster overlapping activity under RedDelta, BRONZE PRESIDENT, Earth Preta (Trend Micro), Stately Taurus (Palo Alto Unit 42), Camaro Dragon (Check Point), and TWILL TYPHOON (Microsoft). In early 2025 a US/FBI-led international law-enforcement operation remotely removed PlugX from thousands of infected US machines; the group responded by retooling rather than disbanding.

## Motivation & Objectives
Pure cyber-espionage: long-term access for intelligence collection, document/email exfiltration, and surveillance of political, diplomatic, and ethnic-minority targets. There is no financial or destructive motive observed. Operations are frequently time-sensitive and event-driven (summits, elections, regional crises), with rapid deployment and exfiltration before infrastructure burn.

## Target Profile
Heavy **APAC and Southeast Asia** focus. Documented targeting includes Myanmar, the Philippines, Thailand, Vietnam, Taiwan, Japan, Singapore, Mongolia, Indonesia, and Pakistan, plus European bodies (notably EU and Vatican-linked diplomacy via RedDelta) and US policy organisations.

**APAC / Australia note:** The TONESHELL backdoor family was first publicly documented in campaigns against **Myanmar, Australia, the Philippines, Japan, and Taiwan**. ASEAN governments and regional foreign ministries are recurring victims. Australian organisations with regional diplomatic, defence, or maritime equities fall squarely within the group's collection remit, and the ACSC (cyber.gov.au) tracks China-nexus espionage actors of this type as a standing threat to Australian government and critical-infrastructure networks. Sectors hit include government/diplomatic, NGOs/think tanks, telecommunications, maritime, religious and ethnic-minority groups, and academia.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
**Initial Access**
- T1566.001 Spearphishing Attachment — malicious archives/LNK/decoy documents with geopolitical lures.
- T1566.002 Spearphishing Link — cloud-hosted (Google Drive, Dropbox) payload links.
- T1091 Replication Through Removable Media — HIUPAN/SnakeDisk USB worms spread PUBLOAD/Yokai.

**Execution & User Interaction**
- T1204.002 Malicious File — victim opens weaponised document/LNK.
- T1218.011 Rundll32 / signed-binary proxy execution of loaders.

**Persistence**
- T1547.001 Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder.
- T1053.005 Scheduled Task/Job.

**Defense Evasion**
- T1574.002 DLL Side-Loading — signed legitimate EXE side-loads malicious DLL (a hallmark TTP).
- T1027 Obfuscated/Encrypted payloads and packers.

**Command & Control**
- T1071.001 Web Protocols (HTTP/HTTPS C2).
- T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer — staged second-stage tooling.

**Exfiltration**
- T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel; staged collection of documents and email.

## Tooling & Malware
- **PlugX** — long-running shared Chinese RAT; persistent backdoor, recon, exfiltration (heavily side-loaded).
- **ToneShell** — modern flagship backdoor, positioned as a PlugX replacement.
- **Hodur** — PlugX variant documented by ESET.
- **PUBLOAD** — downloader/loader, often USB-propagated via HIUPAN.
- **StarProxy** — proxy tool for lateral C2.
- **SnakeDisk** — Thailand-focused USB worm dropping the Yokai backdoor.
- **LOTUSLITE** — 2026 backdoor used against US policy orgs post-PlugX takedown.
- **Cobalt Strike**, Mimikatz, and living-off-the-land utilities for post-exploitation.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2012** — Earliest assessed activity.
- **2017–2019** — BRONZE PRESIDENT / Mongolia, SE Asia government targeting.
- **2020** — RedDelta campaigns against the Vatican and Catholic/EU diplomatic entities.
- **2021–2022** — TONESHELL family debuts vs Myanmar, **Australia**, Philippines, Japan, Taiwan; Hodur documented by ESET.
- **2022–2024** — Earth Preta APAC surge: PUBLOAD + HIUPAN USB worm across SE Asia.
- **Jan 2025** — FBI-led operation remotely deletes PlugX from US victims.
- **2025** — ToneShell/StarProxy arsenal (Zscaler); SnakeDisk/Yokai vs Thailand.
- **2026** — Retooled with LOTUSLITE backdoor and SnakeDisk worm vs US policy organisations (UNC6384 cluster).

## Detection & Hunting
- Hunt for **DLL side-loading**: legitimate signed EXEs loading unsigned/anomalous DLLs from user-writable paths.
- Alert on LNK files spawning rundll32/regsvr32 or executing from archive/temp directories.
- Monitor scheduled-task creation tied to non-standard binaries (`schtasks` anomalies).
- USB-write/autorun telemetry for worm propagation (HIUPAN/SnakeDisk artefacts, `rdmin`/loader files).
- Network: beacon-like HTTP/HTTPS to newly registered or low-reputation domains; cloud-storage payload retrieval.
- YARA/IOC sweeps for PlugX, ToneShell, PUBLOAD loader signatures.

## Mitigations
- Disable/control USB autorun and removable-media execution (device-control policy).
- Application allow-listing and WDAC to break DLL side-loading chains.
- Block macro/LNK execution from email and enforce attachment sandboxing.
- Phishing-resistant MFA and user awareness on geopolitical-themed lures.
- EDR with behavioural detection for side-loading, scheduled-task abuse, and C2 beaconing.
- Egress filtering and DNS/proxy logging to surface low-reputation C2.

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |

## References
- https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0129/
- https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/actor/mustang_panda
- https://www.zscaler.com/blogs/security-research/latest-mustang-panda-arsenal-toneshell-and-starproxy-p1
- https://www.picussecurity.com/resource/blog/breaking-down-mustang-panda-windows-endpoint-campaign
- https://www.cyfirma.com/research/apt-profile-mustang-panda/
- https://cyble.com/threat-actor-profiles/mustang-panda/
- https://www.cyber.gov.au/

apt.body:
# Naikon — APT Profile

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 6 independent sources

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | China (PLA Chengdu MR 2nd TRB, MUCD 78020) |
| Aliases | BRONZE GENEVA, Override Panda, Camerashy, Lotus Panda, Hellsing, PLA Unit 78020 |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0019 |
| First seen | 2010 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage / geopolitical intelligence |

## Overview
Naikon (MITRE **G0019**) is a China-nexus cyber-espionage group active since at least 2010, focused almost exclusively on **government, military, and diplomatic targets across Southeast Asia and the wider APAC region**. It is one of the most regionally specialised Chinese APTs, repeatedly hitting ASEAN member states and regional bodies. Naikon relies on spear phishing, DLL side-loading, and a rotating set of custom backdoors (Aria-body, RainyDay, Nebulae) to maintain long-term access to high-value diplomatic and military networks.

## Attribution & Origin
Naikon is assessed to be a **PLA-attributed** actor linked to the **Chengdu Military Region Second Technical Reconnaissance Bureau (Military Unit Cover Designator 78020)**, a connection first surfaced publicly by ThreatConnect/Defense Group Inc. in the "Camerashy" reporting. Secureworks tracks the cluster as BRONZE GENEVA / BRONZE STERLING. Bitdefender's 2021 "Naikon — Traces from a Military Cyber-Espionage Operation" tied recent activity (Aria-body, RainyDay, Nebulae) to the group with high confidence via shared C2 infrastructure.

## Motivation & Objectives
Strategic geopolitical and military intelligence collection aligned to PRC interests in the South China Sea and Southeast Asian diplomacy. Objectives are long-dwell espionage: harvesting diplomatic communications, military planning documents, and government correspondence from neighbouring states and regional organisations.

## Target Profile
Strongly concentrated on **Southeast Asia / ASEAN**: documented victims include Indonesia, the Philippines, Vietnam, Thailand, Myanmar, Malaysia, Cambodia, Laos, Nepal, and **Australia**, plus international bodies such as the **UNDP** and **ASEAN** secretariat. Target verticals are government ministries (especially foreign affairs), military and defence organisations, and civil government agencies.

**APAC / Australia note:** Naikon's victimology is the most explicitly **APAC/Southeast-Asia-centric** of the major Chinese APTs. Reporting on the 2019–2021 military-org campaign lists targets across SE Asia **and Australia**. For Australian defence, foreign-affairs, and regional-policy organisations — and any entity collaborating with ASEAN governments — Naikon represents a direct, sustained espionage threat. The ACSC (cyber.gov.au) and partners flag China-nexus regional espionage actors of this profile as an enduring risk to government networks.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
**Initial Access**
- T1566.001 Spearphishing Attachment — weaponised documents/archives with government/diplomatic lures.
- T1133 External Remote Services — exploitation/abuse of internet-facing access for entry.

**Execution & User Interaction**
- T1204.002 Malicious File.

**Persistence**
- T1547.001 Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder.
- T1053.005 Scheduled Task/Job.

**Defense Evasion**
- T1574.002 DLL Side-Loading — vulnerable signed EXE + malicious DLL + encrypted payload file (e.g., `rdmin.src` for RainyDay).
- T1055 Process Injection.
- T1027 Obfuscated/Encrypted payloads.

**Credential Access**
- T1003 OS Credential Dumping.

**Command & Control**
- T1071.001 Web Protocols.
- T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer.

**Exfiltration**
- T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel.

## Tooling & Malware
- **Aria-body** (MITRE S0456) — custom modular backdoor used since ~2017; flagship implant.
- **RainyDay** — backdoor deployed via DLL side-loading using the `rdmin.src` encrypted-payload pattern.
- **Nebulae** — backup/secondary backdoor surfaced in the 2021 Bitdefender campaign.
- **RARSTONE / Sys10** — earlier-generation backdoors.
- Naikon legacy backdoor and recon utilities; technique overlaps observed with Cycldek's FoundCore deployment chain (shared side-loading method).

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2010** — Earliest assessed activity.
- **2013–2014** — RARSTONE / SE Asia government campaigns (Trend Micro, Kaspersky).
- **2015** — "Camerashy" report links Naikon to PLA Unit 78020 (ThreatConnect / DGI).
- **~2017** — Aria-body backdoor introduced.
- **2020** — Check Point details Aria-body campaign vs APAC governments and international orgs.
- **Jun 2019 – Mar 2021** — Bitdefender-tracked campaign vs SE Asian military organisations using Aria-body, RainyDay, and the new **Nebulae** backdoor.
- **2022–2026** — Continued regional government/military espionage under BRONZE GENEVA clustering.

## Detection & Hunting
- Hunt the RainyDay side-loading triad: legitimate signed EXE + anomalous DLL + opaque `*.src`/payload file in the same directory.
- DLL side-loading detection: signed binaries loading DLLs from user-writable/unexpected paths.
- Process-injection telemetry and Run-key / scheduled-task persistence anomalies.
- Beacon detection to long-lived or regionally-themed C2 domains; correlate with known Naikon infrastructure.
- Credential-dumping alerts; monitor diplomatic/military file-server access patterns.
- IOC/YARA sweeps for Aria-body, RainyDay, Nebulae.

## Mitigations
- Application allow-listing / WDAC to break DLL side-loading execution chains.
- Phishing-resistant MFA and macro/attachment controls on email gateways.
- Network segmentation isolating diplomatic/military document stores; strict egress filtering.
- EDR with behavioural detection for side-loading, injection, and persistence.
- Harden and monitor external remote services; patch internet-facing assets.
- Threat-intel-driven blocking of known Naikon C2 and proactive hunting in government/defence environments.

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |

## References
- https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0019/
- https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0456/
- https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/actor/naikon
- https://www.bitdefender.com/files/News/CaseStudies/study/396/Bitdefender-PR-Whitepaper-NAIKON-creat5397-en-EN.pdf
- https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/cyberspies-target-military-organizations-with-new-nebulae-backdoor/
- https://www.securityweek.com/chinese-cyberspies-target-military-organizations-asia-new-malware/
- https://www.cyber.gov.au/
