apt.body:
# Salt Typhoon — APT Profile

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 7 independent sources

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | PRC state-sponsored; contractors (incl. sanctioned Sichuan Juxinhe Network Technology) supporting Chinese intelligence services |
| Aliases | GhostEmperor, FamousSparrow, Earth Estries, UNC2286, UNC5807, RedMike, OPERATOR PANDA |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G1045 |
| First seen | 2019 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage (telecom intercept / metadata collection) |

## Overview
Salt Typhoon is a China-nexus cyber-espionage actor active since at least 2019, responsible for numerous compromises of network infrastructure at major US telecom and ISP providers. It is distinguished by **deep exploitation of network-edge devices** (especially Cisco routers), **living-off-the-network-device** persistence (GRE tunnels, loopback manipulation, config/credential dumping), and targeting of **lawful-intercept (CALEA/wiretap) systems** and high-value individuals' communications. MITRE assigned it **G1045** in Feb 2025.

## Attribution & Origin
PRC state-sponsored. The Aug 2025 joint advisory and US Treasury link the activity to **contractors supporting Chinese intelligence services** (MSS, with PLA references).
- **17 Jan 2025** — US Treasury OFAC **sanctioned Sichuan Juxinhe Network Technology Co., Ltd.** for direct involvement with Salt Typhoon.
- The Aug 2025 advisory also names **Beijing Huanyu Tianqiong Information Technology Co., Ltd.** and **Sichuan Zhixin Ruijie Network Technology Co., Ltd.** as providing cyber products/services to China's intelligence services.

## Motivation & Objectives
Cyber-espionage / long-term intelligence collection. Specifically targets **telecom intercept capability**: call detail records and metadata, geolocation, SMS, and in some cases call audio of high-value targets — plus access to **lawful-intercept (CALEA / court-ordered wiretap) systems**.

## Target Profile
Primary sector: **telecommunications providers and ISPs**. US carriers reported breached include **AT&T, Verizon, Lumen, T-Mobile** (reporting cites at least nine US telecoms). Also government, transportation, lodging/hospitality and military networks (per the Aug 2025 advisory). Targets of intelligence value include **lawful-intercept / CALEA wiretap systems** and the communications of high-profile individuals (reporting cites people associated with 2024 US presidential campaigns and government officials).

Regions: United States (epicentre) plus **APAC, EMEA and others** — affected telecoms reported across the US, Italy, a UK-affiliated US telecom, South Africa and Thailand, with earlier Earth Estries victims across a dozen-plus countries (Taiwan, Philippines, India, Indonesia, Malaysia, Vietnam, etc.).

**APAC / Australia relevance — key government guidance.**
- **3 Dec 2024** — CISA/NSA/FBI + **ASD's ACSC (Australia)**, Canada's CCCS and NZ's NCSC-NZ released **"Enhanced Visibility and Hardening Guidance for Communications Infrastructure"** — a joint guide (not a numbered AAxx-xxx advisory) with ~three dozen visibility/hardening best practices for telecom defenders. Cisco and Google Cloud Security contributed.
- **27 Aug 2025** — Multinational Joint Cybersecurity Advisory **AA25-239A**, "Countering Chinese State-Sponsored Actors Compromise of Networks Worldwide to Feed Global Espionage System." Co-sealed by 13+ partners **including Australia (ASD's ACSC)** — confirming APAC relevance — plus Canada (CSE/Cyber Centre), Germany (BfV, BND, BSI), Japan (NCO) and other Five Eyes/EU partners alongside CISA, NSA, FBI. It documents PRC APT targeting of telecom/government/transport/lodging/military and edge routers (backbone, PE and CE routers).

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
Technique IDs verified against the MITRE ATT&CK G1045 page. Techniques marked **(G1045)** are formally attributed on the MITRE page; others are flagged "consistent with reported TTPs" to avoid overstating attribution.

**Initial Access**
- `T1190` Exploit Public-Facing Application *(G1045)* — exploited CVE-2018-0171 (Cisco Smart Install RCE) for initial access; also Cisco IOS XE Web UI CVEs (CVE-2023-20198 / CVE-2023-20273).
- `T1078` Valid Accounts — *consistent with reported TTPs*; used stolen telecom-employee credentials.
- `T1133` External Remote Services — *consistent with reported TTPs*; abuse of remote management/SSH on edge devices.

**Credential Access**
- `T1110.002` Brute Force: Password Cracking *(G1045)* — cracked weakly-encrypted passwords from device config files.

**Persistence**
- `T1098.004` Account Manipulation: SSH Authorized Keys *(G1045)* — added authorized_keys for root/users on Linux-level network devices.
- `T1136` Create Account *(G1045)* — created Linux users via /etc/shadow and /etc/passwd on devices.

**Defense Evasion**
- `T1686` Disable or Modify System Firewall *(G1045)* — modified ACLs and loopback interface addresses.
- `T1685.006` Disable or Modify Tools: Clear Linux/Mac System Logs *(G1045)* — cleared .bash_history, auth.log, lastlog, wtmp, btmp.
- `T1601` Modify System Image (network devices) — *consistent with reported TTPs* (edge-device firmware/config tampering is a hallmark).

**Collection / Discovery**
- `T1040` Network Sniffing *(G1045)* — packet capture between interfaces (via JumbledPath).
- `T1602.002` Data from Configuration Repository: Network Device Config Dump *(G1045)* — dumped device configs to harvest credentials.
- `T1590.004` Gather Victim Network Information: Network Topology *(G1045)* — used configs to map upstream/downstream segments.

**Lateral Movement / C2**
- `T1021.004` Remote Services: SSH *(G1045)* — used a manipulated loopback as SSH source to pivot and bypass ACLs.
- `T1572` Protocol Tunneling *(G1045)* — created GRE tunnels via device config changes.
- `T1071` Application Layer Protocol — *consistent with reported TTPs* (HTTP/S for C2 blending).

**Exfiltration**
- `T1048.003` Exfil Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol *(G1045)* — exfiltrated configs over FTP/TFTP.

**Resource Development**
- `T1587.001` Develop Capabilities: Malware *(G1045)* — built JumbledPath.

## Tooling & Malware
- **Demodex** — Windows kernel-mode rootkit (the original GhostEmperor signature tool; Kaspersky).
- **GhostSpider, Deed RAT / SNAPPYBEE, Crowdoor, SparrowDoor, HemiGate, Zingdoor, TrillClient** — backdoors/stealers in the Earth Estries arsenal (Trend Micro).
- **JumbledPath** (MITRE **S1206**) — custom Go/ELF utility for remote packet capture on Cisco devices via an actor-defined jump-host; also clears logs (Cisco Talos).
- **Living-off-the-network-device:** GRE tunnels for persistence/exfil, loopback-address manipulation to bypass ACLs, native config/credential dumping.
- **Network-edge exploitation:** CVE-2018-0171 (Cisco IOS/IOS XE Smart Install RCE — confirmed by MITRE/Talos), CVE-2023-20198 (IOS XE Web UI auth bypass, CVSS 10.0) and CVE-2023-20273 (priv-esc to root). Much initial access also came from **stolen/valid credentials** rather than only CVEs.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2021** — Kaspersky documents **GhostEmperor** using the Demodex rootkit against Southeast Asian govt/telecom.
- **Nov 2024** — Trend Micro publishes "Game of Emperor," detailing long-term **Earth Estries** intrusions and the GhostSpider/Demodex toolset across 12+ countries.
- **Oct–Nov 2024** — WSJ and others disclose Salt Typhoon breaches of US telecoms (AT&T, Verizon, Lumen; later reported at least nine carriers), including access to wiretap/CALEA systems and comms of political figures.
- **3 Dec 2024** — CISA/NSA/FBI + ACSC/CCCS/NCSC-NZ publish "Enhanced Visibility and Hardening Guidance for Communications Infrastructure."
- **17 Jan 2025** — US Treasury OFAC sanctions **Sichuan Juxinhe Network Technology Co., Ltd.** and an individual, tying the firm to Salt Typhoon.
- **13 Feb 2025** — Recorded Future (Insikt/RedMike) reports exploitation of 1,000+ Cisco IOS XE devices (CVE-2023-20198/20273) Dec 2024–Jan 2025.
- **20 Feb 2025** — Cisco Talos publishes "Weathering the Storm," confirming CVE-2018-0171 use, stolen credentials, and the JumbledPath utility.
- **27 Aug 2025** — 13+ nation multinational advisory **AA25-239A** published, naming associated PRC contractor firms and broadening the targeted-sector picture.

## Detection & Hunting
- **Network-device integrity monitoring:** alert on unauthorised config changes, new local accounts (/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow), added SSH authorized_keys, and loopback-address changes.
- **Config monitoring & diffing:** continuously baseline and diff router/switch configs; flag new GRE tunnels, ACL modifications and unexpected SNMP/TFTP/FTP activity.
- **Log integrity:** monitor for cleared logs (.bash_history, auth.log, wtmp/btmp/lastlog) — gaps are a strong indicator.
- **NetFlow / traffic analysis:** hunt for GRE/tunnelled traffic, config exfil over FTP/TFTP, anomalous device-to-device SSH (especially sourced from loopback), and unexpected packet-capture activity.
- Monitor management-plane access and any access to lawful-intercept/CALEA systems.

## Mitigations
- **Patch and harden Cisco/edge gear:** remediate CVE-2018-0171, CVE-2023-20198, CVE-2023-20273; disable Smart Install and the IOS XE HTTP/HTTPS web UI where not required.
- **Reduce attack surface:** disable unneeded services/protocols (Telnet, unauthenticated SNMP, TFTP); restrict management interfaces to out-of-band/allow-listed hosts.
- **Strong credential hygiene/MFA:** eliminate weak/legacy password encryption, rotate device credentials, enforce phishing-resistant MFA.
- Apply the Dec 2024 Enhanced Visibility & Hardening Guidance and AA25-239A recommendations; centralise/forward logs to tamper-resistant storage.

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |

## References
- MITRE ATT&CK Group G1045 (Salt Typhoon): https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G1045/
- CISA Enhanced Visibility and Hardening Guidance for Communications Infrastructure (Dec 2024): https://www.cisa.gov/resources-tools/resources/enhanced-visibility-and-hardening-guidance-communications-infrastructure
- CISA news release on the Dec 2024 guide: https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/news/cisa-nsa-fbi-and-international-partners-publish-guide-protecting-communications-infrastructure
- CISA Advisory AA25-239A (Aug 2025 multinational advisory): https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-239a
- ASD's ACSC (Australia) listing of the same advisory: https://www.cyber.gov.au/about-us/view-all-content/alerts-and-advisories/countering-chinese-state-sponsored-actors-compromise-of-networks-worldwide-to-feed-global-espionage-system
- US Treasury sanctions press release (Jan 2025): https://home.treasury.gov/news/press-releases/jy2792
- Cisco Talos "Weathering the storm" (Feb 2025): https://blog.talosintelligence.com/salt-typhoon-analysis/
- Trend Micro "Game of Emperor: Unveiling Long Term Earth Estries Cyber Intrusions" (Nov 2024): https://www.trendmicro.com/en_us/research/24/k/earth-estries.html
- Kaspersky/Malpedia GhostEmperor (Demodex): https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/actor/ghostemperor
- MITRE ATT&CK Software S1206 (JumbledPath): https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1206

---
*Profile generated for CyberSec Vault — 2026-06-17*

apt.body:
# Sandworm — APT Profile

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 6 independent sources

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | Russia — GRU Unit 74455 (GTsST) |
| Aliases | Sandworm Team, APT44, Seashell Blizzard (ex-IRIDIUM), Voodoo Bear, ELECTRUM, Telebots |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0034 |
| First seen | 2009 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Sabotage, destruction, espionage, influence |

## Overview
Sandworm Team (designated APT44 by Mandiant since April 2024) is a Russian military-intelligence cyber unit run by the GRU's Main Centre for Special Technologies (GTsST), Military Unit 74455. Unlike its espionage-focused peers, Sandworm is the most operationally mature destructive cyber actor observed to date, specializing in sabotage, critical-infrastructure disruption, and influence operations in support of Russian strategic and wartime objectives.

The group is responsible for the only confirmed cyber-caused power blackouts in history (Ukraine 2015 and 2016), the most financially damaging cyberattack ever (NotPetya, 2017), the disruption of the 2018 Winter Olympics (Olympic Destroyer), and a continuous stream of wiper attacks against Ukraine since the 2022 invasion. Mandiant characterizes APT44 as a "full-spectrum" actor combining espionage, attack, and information operations.

Sandworm pioneered ICS/OT attack tooling (Industroyer/CrashOverride) and routinely abuses Active Directory and Group Policy to mass-deploy wipers across victim networks. Its operations extend beyond Ukraine, with the BadPilot subgroup expanding initial-access operations to the US, UK, Canada, and Australia.

## Attribution & Origin
- **Country / sponsor:** Russia — GRU, Main Centre for Special Technologies (GTsST), Military Unit 74455.
- **US indictment (October 15, 2020):** A Pittsburgh federal grand jury charged six GRU Unit 74455 officers (Andrienko, Detistov, Frolov, Kovalev, Ochichenko, Pliskin) with computer/wire fraud, damaging protected computers, and aggravated identity theft — covering KillDisk/Industroyer, NotPetya, and Olympic Destroyer.
- **Sanctions:** US Treasury sanctioned the GRU and officials in March 2018 (CAATSA / EO 13694) over NotPetya and 2016 election interference; the EU Council imposed cyber-regime sanctions on Unit 74455 personnel in July 2020; the UK (NCSC) publicly attributed Olympic-related attacks in 2020.

## Motivation & Objectives
State-directed sabotage and destruction in support of Russian strategic and wartime objectives. Distinct from espionage-focused peers, Sandworm emphasizes disruptive and destructive effects (wipers, ICS/OT attacks, power-grid blackouts), psychological/influence operations (hack-and-leak, defacement), and degradation of critical infrastructure. Espionage and access operations support these effects.

## Target Profile
**Sectors:** Energy/electric utilities and ICS/OT (primary), government, military/defense, transportation & logistics, telecommunications, maritime, water utilities, media, financial, and elections/political organizations.

**Regions:** Ukraine (dominant focus since 2014, intensified post-Feb 2022), Europe broadly, Georgia, the US (2016 election hack-and-leak, 2018 Olympics infrastructure), France (2017 election), South Korea (2018 PyeongChang Olympics), and the Middle East.

**APAC / Australia targeting:** Explicitly documented.
- The **BadPilot** subgroup (Microsoft Threat Intelligence, Feb 2025) ran a multi-year (2021–2025) initial-access campaign that expanded in 2024 to explicitly include Australia, alongside the US, UK, and Canada — targeting energy, telecommunications, maritime, and arms/defense sectors.
- **ACSC / ASD government advisory:** Australia (ASD's ACSC) co-authored the joint advisory CISA AA22-110A, *"Russian State-Sponsored and Criminal Cyber Threats to Critical Infrastructure"* (April 20, 2022; revised May 9, 2022), produced with US, Canada, NZ, and UK authorities. It names Sandworm/Unit 74455 and its toolset.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK

### Initial Access
- **T1190 – Exploit Public-Facing Application:** Exploits internet-facing servers/appliances for entry (e.g., BadPilot web-shell deployments).
- **T1566.001 – Spearphishing Attachment:** Credible spearphishing (Olympic Destroyer, NotPetya precursor ops) with malicious documents.
- **T1133 – External Remote Services:** Abuses internet-facing access for entry and re-entry.

### Execution
- **T1059.001 / .003 / .005 – Command & Scripting Interpreter (PowerShell / Windows Command Shell / VBScript):** Scripts for staging, lateral movement, and payload execution (VBScripts on a compromised SSH server in the 2016 grid attack).
- **T1204.002 – User Execution: Malicious File:** Relies on victims opening weaponized attachments.

### Persistence
- **T1505.003 – Web Shell:** Deployed Neo-REGEORG on an internet-facing server (2022 grid attack); custom LocalOlive web shell (BadPilot).
- **T1136.002 – Create Account: Domain Account:** Creates domain accounts to retain access.

### Privilege Escalation
- **T1543.003 – Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service:** Replaces a Windows service ImagePath with a backdoor binary (Industroyer ops).
- **T1078 – Valid Accounts:** Reuses harvested valid/admin credentials.

### Defense Evasion
- **T1070.004 – Indicator Removal: File Deletion:** Wipes files and artifacts to cover tracks.
- **T1027 / .002 – Obfuscated Files or Information (incl. Software Packing):** Packs and obfuscates payloads.
- **T1484.001 – Domain Policy Modification: Group Policy Modification:** Abuses GPOs to distribute payloads domain-wide (CaddyWiper msserver.exe and Prestige delivered via Default Domain GPO).

### Credential Access
- **T1003.001 / .003 – OS Credential Dumping (LSASS Memory / NTDS):** Mimikatz + comsvcs.dll to dump credentials from memory (2016 grid attack).
- **T1040 – Network Sniffing:** BlackEnergy sniffer module captured credentials traversing the LAN-to-ICS boundary.

### Discovery
- **T1018 – Remote System Discovery** and **T1083 – File and Directory Discovery:** Maps hosts/shares and enumerates files before deployment.

### Lateral Movement
- **T1021.002 – Remote Services: SMB/Windows Admin Shares** and **T1570 – Lateral Tool Transfer:** Moved tools between corporate and ICS networks (2015 grid attack); used move to push files to network shares.

### Collection
- **T1056.001 – Input Capture: Keylogging:** BlackEnergy keylogger plugin.
- **T1005 – Data from Local System.**

### Command and Control
- **T1071.001 – Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols:** BlackEnergy C2 via HTTP POST.
- **T1572 – Protocol Tunneling** and **T1090 – Proxy:** Tor-based inbound routing (2024).

### Exfiltration
- **T1041 – Exfiltration Over C2 Channel.**

### Impact (destruction-focused)
- **T1485 – Data Destruction:** Core capability — CaddyWiper, SDelete, BlackEnergy KillDisk component, and JUNKMAIL (null-byte overwrite) to wipe files; CaddyWiper wiped OT-related files, mapped drives, and partitions (2022 grid attack).
- **T1486 – Data Encrypted for Impact:** NotPetya and Prestige ransomware encrypt data (Prestige hit transport/logistics in Ukraine and Poland, Oct 2022).
- **T1489 – Service Stop:** Stops services to maximize disruption.
- **T1490 – Inhibit System Recovery:** Prestige deletes backup catalog (wbadmin.exe delete catalog) and volume shadow copies (vssadmin.exe delete shadows /all /quiet); KillDisk corrupts the MBR.
- **T1491.002 – Defacement: External Defacement** and **T1499 – Endpoint Denial of Service:** Website defacement and DoS as influence/disruption effects.
- *ICS ATT&CK (ATT&CK for ICS):* T0826 Loss of Availability, T0827 Loss of Control, T0816 Device Restart/Shutdown, T0831 Manipulation of Control — Industroyer's IEC-104/IEC-101/IEC-61850/OPC DA payloads.

## Tooling & Malware
- **BlackEnergy (S0089):** Modular trojan; KillDisk wiper component, keylogger, network sniffer; used in 2015 grid attack.
- **KillDisk:** Disk-wiping/MBR-corruption component (paired with BlackEnergy/Industroyer ops).
- **Industroyer / CrashOverride (S0604):** First malware purpose-built to manipulate electric-grid ICS protocols (IEC-101/104, IEC-61850, OPC DA); used in 2016 Kyiv blackout.
- **Industroyer2 (2022):** Streamlined variant implementing only IEC-104 to target protection relays in substations; deployed with CaddyWiper.
- **NotPetya / ExPetr (S0368):** Pseudo-ransomware wiper (2017); ~$1B+ damage to indicted victims, global spillover.
- **Olympic Destroyer (S0365):** Self-propagating wiper that disrupted the 2018 PyeongChang Olympics IT.
- **CaddyWiper (S0693):** Data wiper used in the 2022 Ukraine power attack to destroy OT-related files and partitions.
- **VPNFilter (2018):** Modular router/IoT botnet malware (FBI sinkhole disruption, 2018).
- **Cyclops Blink:** VPNFilter successor targeting WatchGuard and ASUS devices; disrupted in 2022 (CISA AA22-054A).
- **Prestige (S1058):** Ransomware against Ukrainian/Polish transport & logistics (Oct 2022).
- **ZEROLOT / AcidPour (2024–2025):** Newer wipers; ZEROLOT against Ukrainian energy firms (Dec 2024–Mar 2025) via AD/GPO abuse.
- **Commodity/utility tooling:** Mimikatz (S0002), Impacket (S0357), Cobalt Strike (S0154), Empire (S0363), PoshC2 (S0378), SDelete (S0195), Invoke-PSImage (S0231), P.A.S. Webshell (S0598), Neo-REGEORG, LocalOlive, plus RMM abuse (Atera Agent, Splashtop) in BadPilot.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **Dec 23, 2015** — Ukraine power grid #1: BlackEnergy + KillDisk; first confirmed cyber-caused blackout (~225k customers). MITRE C0028.
- **Dec 17, 2016** — Ukraine power grid #2 (Kyiv): Industroyer/CrashOverride. MITRE C0025.
- **June 2017** — NotPetya: global destructive pseudo-ransomware seeded via the M.E.Doc supply chain.
- **2017** — French election interference (Macron campaign hack-and-leak).
- **Feb 2018** — Olympic Destroyer: disrupted the PyeongChang Winter Olympics.
- **2018** — VPNFilter router botnet (FBI takedown).
- **2018–2019** — Georgia: mass website defacements and broadcaster disruption.
- **Feb 2022** — Cyclops Blink: WatchGuard/ASUS device botnet exposed/disrupted (CISA AA22-054A).
- **April 2022** — Industroyer2 + CaddyWiper: attempted blackout against a Ukrainian energy provider; thwarted (ESET/CERT-UA). MITRE C0034.
- **Oct 2022** — Prestige ransomware: Ukraine/Poland transport & logistics (Microsoft).
- **April 2024** — Mandiant elevates Sandworm to APT44, consolidating clusters; links to a Texas water-facility intrusion.
- **2023–2025** — Ongoing vs Ukraine + global expansion: BadPilot multi-year access campaign expanding to US/UK/Canada/Australia (2024); ZEROLOT/AcidPour deployments against Ukrainian energy (late 2024–2025); continued AD/GPO-driven destruction, Tor C2, and RMM-tool abuse.

## Detection & Hunting
- **ICS/OT focus:** Monitor for anomalous IEC-104 / IEC-101 / IEC-61850 / OPC DA traffic and unexpected commands to protection relays/RTUs; baseline OT protocol use and alert on deviations; watch for unauthorized config changes and device restart/shutdown commands.
- **GPO abuse (high-value signal):** Alert on Default Domain GPO changes that distribute executables (CaddyWiper msserver.exe, ZEROLOT, Prestige); monitor SYSVOL writes and scheduled-task/service creation pushed via GPO.
- **Recovery-inhibition commands:** Alert on vssadmin delete shadows, wbadmin delete catalog, bcdedit recovery edits, and MBR writes.
- **Credential access:** LSASS access via comsvcs.dll MiniDump, Mimikatz signatures, NTDS.dit extraction.
- **Web shells / external access:** Hunt for Neo-REGEORG, P.A.S., LocalOlive on internet-facing servers; review web-server process trees spawning cmd/PowerShell.
- **RMM/LOLbins:** Unexpected Atera Agent / Splashtop installs; Tor traffic from servers; new domain accounts.
- **Log sources:** Windows Security/Sysmon (4688/1, 4720 account creation, 7045 service install, 4698 scheduled task), GPO change auditing, EDR memory events, OT network sensors (Dragos/Nozomi), NetFlow, web-server logs, AD event correlation.
- **IOCs:** Pull current hashes/IPs from CISA AA22-110A, AA22-054A (Cyclops Blink), ESET Industroyer2 report, Mandiant APT44 appendix; treat IOCs as supplementary to behavior-based detection given rapid wiper turnover.

## Mitigations
- Segment IT/OT with strict, monitored, default-deny boundaries; no direct corporate-to-ICS routing; enforce a DMZ/data-diode model for OT.
- Harden Active Directory & GPO: restrict who can edit GPOs, enable change auditing/alerting, tier admin accounts, use Protected Users / LAPS — this disrupts Sandworm's primary mass-deployment vector.
- Patch internet-facing assets (VPNs, mail/web servers, edge appliances) promptly; replace/patch SOHO/edge devices targeted by Cyclops Blink.
- Phishing-resistant MFA everywhere, especially remote access and admin; disable legacy/default MFA configs.
- Offline, immutable, tested backups; protect VSS and backup infrastructure; rehearse OT recovery.
- Credential hygiene: Credential Guard, disable cached creds where feasible, monitor LSASS access, rotate after incidents.
- Application allowlisting / EDR on OT-adjacent jump hosts; restrict RMM tooling to an approved inventory.
- Adopt the AA22-110A / Shields Up posture and ICS-specific guidance from CISA, ACSC, and NCSC.

## Activity Log

apt.body:
# Scattered Spider — APT Profile

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 9 independent sources

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | Financially-motivated cybercrime collective (English-speaking; US/UK members) |
| Aliases | UNC3944, Octo Tempest, Roasted 0ktapus, Scatter Swine, Storm-0875, Muddled Libra |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G1015 |
| First seen | 2022 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Financial / extortion |

## Overview
Scattered Spider (MITRE **G1015**) is a large, loosely affiliated, **financially motivated** cybercrime collective active since at least 2022. Unlike the China-nexus espionage groups, it is **native-English-speaking** (members largely in the US and UK, linked to the broader "Com" / "Comm" online ecosystem) and is defined by **best-in-class social engineering** rather than custom malware. The group specialises in help-desk impersonation, MFA-fatigue push bombing, and SIM-swap attacks to seize identities, then pivots to data theft, extortion, and ransomware deployment.

## Attribution & Origin
Scattered Spider is **not state-sponsored** — it is a criminal collective. It is tracked as UNC3944 (Mandiant/Google), Octo Tempest (Microsoft), Roasted 0ktapus/Oktapus (Group-IB, after the 0ktapus phishing kit), Scatter Swine (Okta), Storm-0875, and Muddled Libra (Palo Alto Unit 42). Members are predominantly young, English-speaking individuals; multiple arrests have occurred in the US and UK. CISA/FBI issued joint advisory **AA23-320A** (Nov 16, 2023; updated July 29, 2025) detailing its TTPs.

## Motivation & Objectives
Purely **financial**: monetisation via data-theft extortion and ransomware. The group has affiliated with ransomware-as-a-service operations including **ALPHV/BlackCat** and later **DragonForce**, deploying encryptors after exfiltrating data for double extortion. Targets are chosen for leverage and payout potential.

## Target Profile
Primarily **US and UK** organisations, with documented activity against **Canada, Australia, and Western Europe**. Early waves hit telecommunications and BPO firms (for SIM-swap and downstream access); later campaigns devastated **gaming/casino & hospitality** (notably the 2023 MGM Resorts and Caesars Entertainment intrusions), and expanded into technology, financial services, retail, insurance, and cloud/SaaS providers.

**APAC / Australia note:** While its centre of gravity is the English-speaking US/UK market, Scattered Spider has targeted **Australian** organisations, and the **ACSC (cyber.gov.au) published a dedicated Scattered Spider advisory** mirroring the CISA/FBI guidance — a clear signal of relevance to Australian enterprises, particularly those with large help-desk/BPO operations or consumer-facing identity flows.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
**Initial Access — Social Engineering (core strength)**
- T1566.004 Spearphishing Voice (vishing) — impersonating IT/help-desk staff to drive credential/MFA resets.
- T1660 Phishing (smishing) — SMS lures with org-specific look-alike domains (0ktapus-style kits).
- **SIM swapping** — hijacking victim phone numbers to intercept SMS OTP/MFA (mapped under credential/MFA compromise).

**Credential Access & MFA Bypass**
- T1621 Multi-Factor Authentication Request Generation — MFA fatigue / push bombing.
- T1556.006 Modify Authentication Process: MFA — registering attacker-controlled MFA tokens.
- T1606 Forge Web Credentials — session/token abuse.

**Persistence & Privilege**
- T1136 Create Account — creating their own user/admin accounts.
- T1098.005 Account Manipulation: Device Registration — enrolling attacker devices.
- T1078 Valid Accounts — using stolen/legitimate credentials.

**Execution & Defense Evasion — Living off legitimate tools**
- T1219 Remote Access Software — AnyDesk, TeamViewer, ScreenConnect, Splashtop, Splashtop pushed onto victims.

**Trusted Relationship**
- T1199 Trusted Relationship — compromising third-party/SaaS providers to reach multiple downstream targets.

**Impact & Exfiltration**
- T1567 Exfiltration Over Web Service — data theft to attacker cloud/storage.
- T1486 Data Encrypted for Impact — ALPHV/BlackCat then DragonForce ransomware.

## Tooling & Malware
Scattered Spider is a **"living-off-the-land" / legitimate-tooling** actor, not a malware developer:
- **Remote access:** AnyDesk, TeamViewer, ScreenConnect (ConnectWise), Splashtop, Pulseway, ngrok tunnelling.
- **Credential/info-stealers:** RACCOON Stealer, VIDAR Stealer, Mimikatz.
- **RATs:** RattyRAT (Java RAT) in some intrusions.
- **Ransomware (affiliate):** ALPHV/BlackCat (earlier), **DragonForce** (later).
- **Phishing kits:** 0ktapus / EIGHTBAIT-style credential-harvest kits with org-branded domains.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2022** — Emergence; 0ktapus SMS phishing campaign compromises 100+ orgs (telecom/BPO focus) for SIM-swap and downstream access.
- **2023** — High-profile help-desk-driven intrusions; **MGM Resorts** and **Caesars Entertainment** (Sept 2023) — vishing the help desk, ALPHV/BlackCat deployment, major operational/financial impact.
- **Nov 16, 2023** — CISA/FBI joint advisory **AA23-320A** published.
- **2024** — Multiple arrests (US/UK); shift in RaaS affiliation; continued SaaS/cloud and finance targeting.
- **Jul 29, 2025** — AA23-320A advisory updated with expanded TTPs and **DragonForce** ransomware association.
- **2025–2026** — Sustained help-desk/identity-centric extortion across retail, insurance, aviation, and tech sectors.

## Detection & Hunting
- **Help-desk abuse:** alert on credential/MFA reset requests, especially out-of-band or for privileged accounts; correlate with inbound calls. Enforce and log identity-verification challenges.
- **MFA anomalies:** new MFA device/token registrations (T1556.006, T1098.005), bursts of push prompts (T1621), logins immediately following resets.
- **SIM-swap indicators:** sudden MFA-delivery changes, carrier-side number ports, OTP delivery to new devices.
- **Remote access tools:** detect installation/execution of AnyDesk, ScreenConnect, TeamViewer, Splashtop, ngrok where not sanctioned (T1219).
- **New account creation** and privilege escalation in IdP (Okta/Entra ID) audit logs (T1136).
- **Impossible-travel / atypical geo** logins on cloud/SaaS; session-token reuse anomalies.

## Mitigations
- **Phishing-resistant MFA** (FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware keys) — removes push-bombing and OTP-interception vectors.
- **Harden help-desk identity verification** — out-of-band, multi-factor proof before any password/MFA reset; supervisor approval for privileged resets.
- **Restrict / allow-list remote access software**; block unsanctioned RMM via application control.
- **Limit SIM-swap exposure** — remove SMS as an MFA factor; carrier port-out PINs.
- **Tighten IdP controls** — monitor MFA enrolment, conditional access, device registration; least privilege for admins (PAM).
- **Third-party/SaaS risk management** to counter trusted-relationship abuse (T1199).
- **Immutable/offline backups** and rapid IR plan for ransomware (T1486).

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.
- 2026-06-22 — **TfL attack — two members plead guilty.** Two members of Scattered Spider (described as a sub-group of the ALPHV ransomware ecosystem) pleaded guilty to the **Transport for London (TfL)** cyberattack: **Thalha Jubair (20)** and **Owen Flowers (18)**. Flowers had initially been released from custody on strict conditions. (Source: @vxunderground, X — 2026-06-22; corroborated by mainstream coverage "Two Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty in TfL Cyberattack".)

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2026-06-22 | Law enforcement / prosecution | Two Scattered Spider members (Thalha Jubair, 20; Owen Flowers, 18) plead guilty to the TfL cyberattack | @vxunderground (X) |

## References
- https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G1015/
- https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa23-320a
- https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/2023-11/aa23-320a_scattered_spider_0.pdf
- https://www.ic3.gov/CSA/2025/250729.pdf
- https://www.cyber.gov.au/about-us/view-all-content/alerts-and-advisories/scattered-spider
- https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1566/004/
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scattered_Spider

---
*Profile generated for CyberSec Vault — 2026-06-17*

## Tags
 #verified

### Activity Log — 2026-07-03/04: Member extradited to US
An alleged Scattered Spider member (dual US/Estonian citizen; separately reported as 19-year-old **Peter Stokes**, arrested at a Finnish airport) was **extradited to the United States** to face charges. Scattered Spider has been tied to 100+ intrusions.
> Source: BleepingComputer (2026-07-02) + X @TodayCyberNews (2026-07-03)

---

## Activity Log — 2026-07-06: Microsoft Assisted FBI Identification of Peter Stokes via GDID
Court files reportedly reveal that **Microsoft helped the FBI identify/track Scattered Spider member Peter "Bouquet" Stokes** using a **GDID — a Global Device Identifier assigned to Windows devices**. This follows the earlier (2026-07-03) extradition of Stokes to the US. Notable for attribution tradecraft: device-identifier telemetry as a deanonymization vector against the group.

> Source: Com Feed (@thecomfeed, X) — 2026-07-05 (quoted via @hackerfantastic); relates to prior BleepingComputer extradition report.

*Appended via x-bugbounty-intel-sweep — 2026-07-06*

apt.body:
# Turla — APT Profile

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 7 independent sources

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | Russia — FSB Center 16 |
| Aliases | Snake, Uroburos, Venomous Bear, Secret Blizzard, Waterbug, KRYPTON |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0010 |
| First seen | 2004 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage |

## Overview
Turla is a long-running Russian state cyber-espionage group attributed to a unit within Center 16 of the FSB. Active since at least 2004 (with tooling lineage tracing to ~2003), it is one of the most technically sophisticated and stealthy intrusion sets ever observed, distinguished by extreme patience, multi-year dwell times, highly selective targeting of high-value victims, and aggressive anti-attribution tradecraft.

Turla pioneered unusual techniques including satellite-based C2 (hijacking one-way downstream satellite IPs to anonymize command-and-control), hijacking other threat actors' infrastructure and tooling (Iranian OilRig in 2019; Pakistani Storm-0156 and the Amadey crimeware botnet in 2024–25), and operating at the ISP level for adversary-in-the-middle attacks (ApolloShadow, 2025). It is publicly linked to the 2008 Agent.btz worm that breached US CENTCOM.

The group is not financially motivated and runs no destructive or ransomware operations; its mission is long-term intelligence collection on behalf of the Russian state. Its flagship Snake implant was disrupted by the FBI's Operation MEDUSA in May 2023.

## Attribution & Origin
- **Country / sponsor:** Russia — FSB, specifically a unit within Center 16 (the FSB's signals-intelligence directorate). The US Government attributes Snake malware operations directly to "a known unit within Center 16 of the FSB."
- **Operation MEDUSA (May 9, 2023):** Court-authorized FBI operation that disrupted the global Snake P2P network using a purpose-built tool named PERSEUS, which forced Snake implants to overwrite their own vital components. Announced by DOJ (EDNY) with joint advisory AA23-129A.
- **NSA/NCSC joint advisory (Oct 21, 2019):** Exposed Turla's hijacking of Iranian APT34/OilRig infrastructure.
- **Sanctions note:** No standalone OFAC designation is publicly tied specifically to the "Turla/Center 16" label; the primary public countermeasures are the legal/technical disruption (Operation MEDUSA, May 2023) plus repeated multinational attribution.

## Motivation & Objectives
Long-term cyber-espionage and intelligence collection on behalf of the Russian state. Hallmarks: extreme stealth, patience, and operational longevity (years of dwell time), highly selective targeting of high-value victims, and aggressive anti-attribution tradecraft (satellite C2, hijacking other actors' infrastructure/tools). Not financially motivated; no destructive/ransomware mission.

## Target Profile
**Sectors:** Government, ministries of foreign affairs, embassies/diplomatic missions, military and defense contractors, intelligence agencies, research/education, pharmaceutical, and journalists. Victims span 50+ countries.

**Regions:** Primarily NATO members, Europe, and former Soviet states, but the Snake infrastructure footprint is global.

**APAC / Australia targeting:** Australia is directly implicated.
- The May 2023 joint Snake advisory (CISA AA23-129A) states Snake infrastructure was found across North America, South America, Europe, Africa, Asia, and Australia.
- ACSC/ASD co-sealed the Snake advisory. Australia's ACSC (part of ASD) was a co-authoring agency on the 2023 Snake/Operation MEDUSA joint advisory alongside FBI, NSA, CISA, CNMF, Canada's CCCS/CSE, and New Zealand's NCSC — confirming Australian government concern about Turla targeting in the region.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK

### Initial Access
- **T1189 – Drive-by Compromise:** Infects victims via watering holes (compromised diplomatic/government-relevant websites).
- **T1566 / T1204.001 – Phishing → User Execution (Malicious Link):** Spearphishing with links to deliver first-stage droppers.

### Execution
- **T1059.001 / .003 / .005 / .006 / .007 – Command & Scripting Interpreter:** Uses PowerShell, cmd.exe, VBScript, IronPython (IronNetInjector), and multiple JavaScript backdoors.

### Persistence
- **T1547.004 – Boot/Logon Autostart (Winlogon Helper DLL):** Adds a Shell value under HKCU\...\Winlogon.
- **T1546.003 / .013 – Event Triggered Execution:** WMI event subscriptions and malicious PowerShell profiles for persistence.

### Privilege Escalation
- **T1068 – Exploitation for Privilege Escalation:** Exploited the VBoxDrv.sys (VirtualBox driver) vulnerability for kernel-mode privileges (BYOVD).
- **T1134.002 – Access Token Manipulation (Create Process with Token):** RPC backdoors impersonate/steal tokens before running commands.

### Defense Evasion
- **T1027.011 – Obfuscated Files/Info (Fileless Storage):** Stores encrypted/encoded payloads in the Registry.
- **T1112 – Modify Registry:** Stores payloads in registry values; uses Reg (S0075).
- **T1055 / .001 – Process Injection / Reflective DLL Injection:** Reflective PE/PowerShell injection (PowerSploit, Metasploit).

### Credential Access
- **T1555.004 – Credentials from Windows Credential Manager.**
- **T1110 – Brute Force:** net use against systems with a predefined password list.
- **T1588.002 – Obtain Capabilities (Tool):** Customizes Mimikatz (S0002).

### Discovery
- **T1087.002 – Account Discovery (Domain):** net user /domain.
- **T1082 – System Information Discovery:** systeminfo, set.
- **T1615 – Group Policy Discovery:** gpresult.
- *(Plus T1007 tasklist /svc, T1124 net time, T1069.002 net group "Domain Admins" /domain.)*

### Lateral Movement
- **T1021.002 – Remote Services (SMB/Admin Shares):** net use to connect to lateral systems.
- **T1570 – Lateral Tool Transfer:** RPC backdoors move files across the internal network.

### Collection
- **T1560.001 – Archive Collected Data (via Utility):** RARs USB-drive data before exfil.
- **T1025 – Data from Removable Media** / **T1005 – Data from Local System.**

### Command and Control
- **T1102 / .002 – Web Service / Bidirectional Communication:** Abuses Pastebin, Dropbox, GitHub, and Google Apps Script as C2.
- **T1090 / .001 – Proxy / Internal Proxy:** Compromises internal hosts to relay traffic to C2 (also UPnP RPC proxies).
- **T1584.006 – Compromise Infrastructure (Web Services):** Frequently hijacks compromised WordPress sites for C2.
- **T1071.003 – Application Layer Protocol (Mail):** Backdoors (e.g., LightNeuron) receive commands via email attachments.

### Exfiltration
- **T1583.006 – Acquire Infrastructure: Web Services:** Document exfiltration via Dropbox/GitHub web accounts.

> **Signature tradecraft to flag:** satellite-based C2 ("satlink hijacking," since ~2007 per Kaspersky — hijacks one-way downstream satellite IPs to anonymize C2); hijacking other actors' infrastructure (Iranian OilRig/APT34 in 2019; Pakistani Storm-0156 and the Amadey MaaS botnet in 2024–25); and watering-hole compromises (T1189).

## Tooling & Malware
- **Snake / Uroburos (S0022):** Flagship modular kernel-mode rootkit/espionage platform; cross-platform (Windows/Linux/macOS).
- **ComRAT (S0126):** Second-stage implant descended from Agent.btz; latest versions use the Gmail web UI for C2.
- **Kazuar (S0265):** Multi-platform .NET espionage backdoor with an extensive API; updated version deployed in Ukraine 2024.
- **Carbon (S0335):** Modular second-stage backdoor framework (successor to earlier Uroburos components).
- **Mosquito:** JavaScript/installer backdoor delivered via fake Flash installers; later pivoted toward generic tools (Metasploit).
- **LightNeuron:** First known Microsoft Exchange Transport Agent backdoor; reads/modifies/blocks all mail, controlled via steganographic PDF/JPG email attachments (active since ~2014).
- **Crutch (S0538):** Backdoor using Dropbox for exfiltration of documents/USB data.
- **TinyTurla (S0668):** Lightweight backup/second-chance backdoor (service DLL); TinyTurla-NG variant later seen vs. Ukraine.
- **Penquin / Penquin Turla (S0587):** Linux backdoor/RAT (BPF-based covert wake-up), active since ~2014.
- **Gazer (S0168):** Second-stage backdoor used against embassies/consulates from ~2016.
- **Tavdig (Wipbot/Epic):** Loader/backdoor used to chain into Kazuar in the 2024 Amadey campaign.
- **ApolloShadow (2025):** Newest custom malware; installs a rogue root certificate and enables TLS interception in the ISP-level AiTM embassy campaign.
- **Customized commodity tools:** Mimikatz, Metasploit/Meterpreter, PowerSploit.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2008** — Agent.btz: USB worm breaches US CENTCOM ("worst breach of US military computers in history"); triggered Operation Buckshot Yankee and the creation of US Cyber Command. Later code-linked to Turla.
- **2007–ongoing** — Satellite C2 hijacking: Kaspersky (2015) exposed Turla hiding C2 behind hijacked satellite downlink IPs.
- **2014** — RUAG (Switzerland): long-running intrusion into a Swiss state-owned defense firm; ~23 GB exfiltrated (disclosed 2016).
- **~2014–2019** — LightNeuron: Exchange Transport-Agent backdoor vs. an Eastern-European MFA and a Middle East diplomatic body (ESET, May 2019).
- **2019** — OilRig/APT34 infrastructure hijack: NSA/NCSC joint advisory (Oct 2019) — Turla took over Iranian APT34 tools and C2, scanning across 35+ countries.
- **2020–2021** — ComRAT v4 / Crutch / TinyTurla: continued diplomatic and government targeting; novel cloud-service C2.
- **May 9, 2023** — Operation MEDUSA / Snake takedown: FBI's PERSEUS tool disables the global Snake P2P network; joint advisory AA23-129A co-sealed by Five Eyes (incl. ACSC/ASD).
- **Nov 2022–2024** — Storm-0156 piggybacking: Microsoft (Dec 2024) — Secret Blizzard compromised Pakistan-based Storm-0156's C2 to spy on South Asian government/military targets.
- **Mar–Apr 2024** — Amadey MaaS abuse (Ukraine): commandeered the Amadey crimeware botnet to drop Tavdig → updated Kazuar onto Ukrainian military devices.
- **Feb–Jul 2025** — ApolloShadow / ISP-level AiTM (embassies in Moscow): Microsoft (Jul 31, 2025) — first confirmed Turla capability to operate at the ISP level, hijacking Windows captive-portal/connectivity checks to push a rogue root cert and intercept TLS against foreign diplomats in Russia.

## Detection & Hunting
- **Process/command-line telemetry:** Hunt the recon cluster: systeminfo, gpresult, tasklist /svc, net user /domain, net group "Domain Admins" /domain, net accounts /domain, fsutil fsinfo drives, net time, tracert — especially in rapid succession from one host (classic Turla check-in survey).
- **Registry:** Watch for new Shell values under HKCU\...\Winlogon; large/encoded blobs written to registry (fileless payload storage, T1027.011).
- **Persistence:** Audit WMI event consumers/filters and modifications to PowerShell profile files.
- **Exchange:** Inventory and validate all Transport Agents (LightNeuron); alert on unsigned/unexpected agents and outbound mail anomalies.
- **Network/C2:** Anomalous use of Pastebin/Dropbox/GitHub/Google Apps Script by server or service accounts; outbound to compromised WordPress sites; odd traffic to one-way satellite IP ranges; for the 2025 vector, monitor captive-portal redirects, unexpected new root certificates, firewall-rule changes, and new hidden local admin accounts.
- **Drivers:** Detect VBoxDrv.sys load on systems without VirtualBox (BYOVD / T1068).
- **Credential tooling:** Mimikatz/Meterpreter behavioral detections; reflective DLL/PE injection.
- **IOCs/signatures:** Use CISA AA23-129A (Snake host-file artifacts, named-pipe/HTTP signatures, Snort/YARA) and ESET/Kaspersky/Microsoft hashes for ComRAT, Kazuar, Crutch, TinyTurla, Penquin, ApolloShadow. Treat IOCs as supplementary — Turla rotates infra heavily; prioritize behavior.

## Mitigations
- Patch aggressively (especially internet-facing servers and Exchange); restrict/monitor driver loading and enable HVCI/driver block-lists to counter BYOVD.
- Eliminate shared local-admin passwords (LAPS) — Turla abuses identical local creds for lateral movement.
- Enforce MFA, least privilege, and tiered admin; restrict outbound access from servers to cloud file/code services used as C2.
- Network segmentation and egress filtering; inspect/limit anomalous satellite or captive-portal traffic.
- Constrain PowerShell (Constrained Language Mode, logging, AMSI); enable script-block + module logging.
- Harden Exchange: audit Transport Agents; deploy EDR on mail servers.
- Validate the root-certificate store and alert on new trusted roots (counters the 2025 TLS-interception vector).
- Apply mitigations in CISA AA23-129A; use it for retro-hunting Snake.

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only: future intel sweeps add dated entries below -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |

## References
- MITRE ATT&CK G0010: https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0010/
- CISA AA23-129A (Hunting Russian "Snake" Malware): https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa23-129a
- Snake CSA PDF: https://media.defense.gov/2023/May/09/2003218554/-1/-1/1/JOINT_CSA_HUNTING_RU_INTEL_SNAKE_MALWARE_20230509.PDF
- US DOJ — Operation MEDUSA / Snake disruption: https://www.justice.gov/usao-edny/pr/justice-department-announces-court-authorized-disruption-snake-malware-network
- NSA/NCSC 2019 advisory (Iranian infra hijack): https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/news/turla-group-exploits-iran-apt-to-expand-coverage-of-victims
- NCSC UK Snake exposure (lists ACSC as partner): https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/news/uk-and-allies-expose-snake-malware-threat-from-russian-cyber-actors
- ACSC / ASD (Australia): https://www.cyber.gov.au/
- Microsoft — Secret Blizzard / Storm-0156 (Dec 2024): https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2024/12/04/frequent-freeloader-part-i-secret-blizzard-compromising-storm-0156-infrastructure-for-espionage/
- Microsoft — ApolloShadow / ISP-level AiTM (Jul 2025): https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2025/07/31/frozen-in-transit-secret-blizzards-aitm-campaign-against-diplomats/
- ESET — LightNeuron whitepaper: https://web-assets.esetstatic.com/wls/2019/05/ESET-LightNeuron.pdf
- Kaspersky — Satellite Turla: https://securelist.com/satellite-turla-apt-command-and-control-in-the-sky/72081/

*Profile generated for CyberSec Vault — 2026-06-17*

## Tags
 #verified

## Activity Log — 2026-06-26
- **STOCKSTAY .NET backdoor** attributed to Turla by Google/Mandiant; espionage vs **Ukraine government & military**. Overlaps with **Kazuar**. Delivery via phishing → .rdp files, MSI installers, and **WinRAR CVE-2025-8088** lures. See [[STOCKSTAY-Turla-DotNet-Backdoor-2026]].

## Trend Tracking — 2026-06-26
- Turla continuing Ukraine-focused espionage; tooling evolution (new .NET implant STOCKSTAY alongside legacy Kazuar). Reuse of commodity-style delivery (RDP/MSI/WinRAR lures) for a state APT.

## Activity Log — 2026-07-14
- **FSB Center 16 critical-infrastructure / router advisory (Jul 13 2026):** FBI/CISA/NSA + 8 allies issued a joint Cybersecurity Advisory — *"Improve Router Hygiene to Protect Against Russian State-Sponsored Targeting"* — warning that **FSB Center 16** actors continue exploiting poorly configured routers/networking devices across critical-infrastructure sectors. This targets Center 16's ICS/critical-infra sub-clusters (Berserk Bear / Static Tundra / Dragonfly) rather than the classic Turla espionage set, but shares the same FSB Center 16 parent unit. See [[Russian-FSB-Center16-Router-Critical-Infra-Advisory-2026]].
- Same window: **EU + UK first joint cyber-sanctions package** targeting Russian GRU hackers/entities (Jul 13 2026).

## Trend Tracking — 2026-07-14
- Renewed multinational public pressure on **FSB Center 16**: router/edge-device hygiene framed as the primary defensive lever against Russian state targeting of critical infrastructure. Center 16 activity spans both espionage (Turla/Snake) and critical-infra (Berserk Bear/Static Tundra) missions.

apt.body:
# Volt Typhoon — APT Profile

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 6 independent sources

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | PRC state-sponsored |
| Aliases | BRONZE SILHOUETTE, Vanguard Panda, DEV-0391, UNC3236, Voltzite, Insidious Taurus, DazedToad |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G1017 |
| First seen | 2021 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage + Pre-positioning for sabotage/disruption |

## Overview
Volt Typhoon is a PRC state-sponsored actor and the **canonical living-off-the-land (LOTL) threat group**. Active since at least mid-2021, it focuses on **pre-positioning** within US and allied critical-infrastructure IT networks to enable disruptive or destructive cyberattacks during a future major crisis or conflict (e.g. an Indo-Pacific / Taiwan contingency), rather than near-term data theft. It uses minimal or no custom malware on victim IT networks, abusing built-in Windows tools to blend with normal admin activity. Associated edge-device botnet activity is tracked as MITRE Campaign **C0035 (KV Botnet)**.

## Attribution & Origin
PRC state-sponsored / state-directed. The Feb 2024 advisory assesses the actors are **pre-positioning to be able to launch destructive or disruptive attacks against US critical infrastructure in the event of a major crisis or conflict with the United States** — a strategic shift from traditional espionage.

## Motivation & Objectives
A blend of espionage/information-gathering and — primarily — **pre-positioning for sabotage/disruption**: establishing long-dwell persistent access (in some cases ~5 years) for potential future destructive operations against critical infrastructure.

## Target Profile
Sectors: US critical infrastructure — **communications, energy (power), water/wastewater, transportation** — plus manufacturing, maritime, government, IT, education and construction. Regions: continental US and territories, with notable early targeting of **Guam** (a key US Indo-Pacific military hub).

**APAC / Australia relevance.**
- Microsoft (24 May 2023) first publicly named the actor and detailed Guam/US critical-infrastructure targeting via LOTL.
- CISA/NSA/FBI joint advisory **AA24-038A (7 Feb 2024)** confirmed persistent access (some ~5 years) across multiple critical-infrastructure sectors. It was **co-sealed by the full Five Eyes: US (CISA, NSA, FBI), Australia (ASD's ACSC), Canada (CCCS), UK (NCSC-UK), New Zealand (NCSC-NZ).**
- The earlier advisory **AA23-144A** was also co-authored with **ACSC**. The Five Eyes co-sealing makes the guidance directly relevant to Australian and broader APAC critical-infrastructure operators (e.g. under Australia's SOCI Act regime).

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
Technique IDs verified against the MITRE ATT&CK G1017 page. **LOTL is the defining characteristic** — minimal malware, heavy abuse of native binaries.

**Initial Access**
- `T1190` Exploit Public-Facing Application — exploited internet-facing appliances (e.g. Fortinet/edge devices).
- `T1133` External Remote Services — leveraged externally-facing remote services.

**Execution (LOTL)**
- `T1059.001` Command & Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell — hands-on-keyboard discovery/execution.
- `T1059.003` Windows Command Shell — cmd.exe for discovery and operations.
- `T1047` Windows Management Instrumentation — WMI/wmic for remote execution and discovery.

**Credential Access**
- `T1003.003` OS Credential Dumping: NTDS — used `ntdsutil` to create install media containing AD usernames and password hashes from ntds.dit.
- `T1003.001` OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory — dumped LSASS for credentials.
- `T1552.004` Unsecured Credentials: Private Keys — read Chrome Local State AES key to decrypt stored passwords.

**Defense Evasion (LOTL)**
- `T1070.007` Indicator Removal: Clear Network Connection History — edited/inspected server logs to remove their IPs.
- `T1036.005` Masquerading: Match Legitimate Name/Location — renamed FRP/Earthworm binaries (e.g. cisco_up.exe, vm3dservice.exe, WmiPreSV.exe).

**Discovery**
- `T1018` Remote System Discovery — enumerated other hosts on the network.
- `T1016` System Network Configuration Discovery — mapped topology (e.g. via ping).

**Lateral Movement / Persistence**
- `T1078.002` Valid Accounts: Domain Accounts — reused compromised domain credentials.
- `T1021.001` Remote Services: RDP — RDP with valid accounts for lateral movement.

**Command & Control (LOTL)**
- `T1090.001 / T1090.003` Proxy: Internal Proxy (`netsh interface portproxy`) and Multi-hop Proxy — proxied traffic via netsh and through compromised SOHO devices.

**Resource Development**
- `T1584.005` Compromise Infrastructure: Botnet — Cisco/NETGEAR EOL SOHO routers running KV Botnet malware (C0035).
- `T1584.008` Compromise Infrastructure: Network Devices — compromised SOHO edge devices to relay/obfuscate traffic.

## Tooling & Malware
Volt Typhoon relies on **built-in, legitimate tools** rather than custom implants:
- **Native / LOLBins:** `netsh` (notably `netsh interface portproxy`), `wmic`/WMI, PowerShell, the Windows command shell, `ntdsutil` (dump ntds.dit), `ping`, `net` (group/localgroup), `dnscmd`.
- **Credential access:** Mimikatz and direct LSASS access; theft of ntds.dit plus SYSTEM/SECURITY registry hives; harvesting of browser-stored credentials and the Chrome Local State AES key.
- **Off-the-shelf tools (often renamed):** Fast Reverse Proxy (FRP) and Earthworm for tunneling/proxying.
- **Infrastructure/obfuscation:** compromised SOHO routers and edge devices (EOL Cisco and NETGEAR) implanted with **KV Botnet** malware (C0035) to proxy/relay traffic and conceal PRC origin; also compromised PRTG servers and VPS/multi-hop proxies for C2.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **24 May 2023** — Microsoft publishes "Volt Typhoon targets US critical infrastructure with living-off-the-land techniques," first public naming and Guam/critical-infrastructure disclosure.
- **24 May 2023** — CISA/NSA/FBI + Five Eyes (incl. ACSC) release joint advisory **AA23-144A** ("PRC State-Sponsored Cyber Actor Living off the Land to Evade Detection").
- **Dec 2023 (court authorisation) / Jan 2024** — FBI/DOJ court-authorised operation to disrupt the **KV Botnet** of compromised US SOHO routers used by Volt Typhoon.
- **31 Jan 2024** — FBI Director Christopher Wray testifies to the House Select Committee on the CCP, confirming the KV Botnet takedown and warning of pre-positioning to "wreak havoc" on US critical infrastructure.
- **7 Feb 2024** — CISA/NSA/FBI + Five Eyes release joint advisory **AA24-038A**, confirming long-dwell persistent access and pre-positioning for disruptive/destructive attacks; supplemental LOTL guidance and MAR-10448362-1.v1 (AR24-038A).

## Detection & Hunting
LOTL is the core challenge — minimal malware artifacts mean signature/IOC detection is largely ineffective. Shift to behavioral analytics:
- Baseline normal admin behavior and alert on deviations.
- Hunt suspicious native-tool use: `ntdsutil.exe`/`vssadmin` on domain controllers; `netsh interface portproxy` additions; anomalous `wmic`/PowerShell command lines; `net group`/`net localgroup` recon bursts.
- Monitor for ntds.dit, SYSTEM and SECURITY hive copies written to `C:\Windows\Temp\`, or files with mismatched extensions (e.g. `.gif` appended to ntds.dit).
- Watch for unusual authentication from valid/domain accounts (off-hours, new source hosts, impossible travel) and unexpected RDP/lateral movement.
- Inspect edge/SOHO devices for proxy/relay behavior; assume log deletion (`T1070`).
- Enable, centralise and retain comprehensive logging (PowerShell script-block + module logging, command-line process auditing, Security and DC logs) — long retention is critical given multi-year dwell and local log clearing.

## Mitigations
- Patch and **replace end-of-life / internet-facing edge devices** (routers, VPNs, firewalls); apply vendor updates to public-facing apps promptly.
- Enforce **phishing-resistant MFA**, especially for remote access and administrative/domain accounts; enforce least privilege and rotate credentials after suspected exposure.
- Harden domain controllers; restrict and monitor `netsh portproxy` usage; segment networks and restrict lateral movement (RDP, SMB).
- Secure and centralise logs off-host with extended retention; protect against log clearing.

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |

## References
- CISA AA24-038A: https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa24-038a
- CISA AA23-144A: https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa23-144a
- Microsoft Volt Typhoon blog (24 May 2023): https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2023/05/24/volt-typhoon-targets-us-critical-infrastructure-with-living-off-the-land-techniques/
- MITRE ATT&CK G1017: https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G1017/
- NSA/CISA Joint CSA "Living off the Land" PDF (AA23-144A): https://media.defense.gov/2023/May/24/2003229517/-1/-1/0/CSA_Living_off_the_Land.PDF
- AA24-038A full CSA PDF: https://media.defense.gov/2024/Feb/07/2003389935/-1/-1/0/CSA-PRC-Compromise-US-Critical-Infrastructure.PDF
- DOJ press release (KV Botnet disruption): https://www.justice.gov/archives/opa/pr/us-government-disrupts-botnet-peoples-republic-china-used-conceal-hacking-critical
- FBI Director Wray statement to House Select Committee on the CCP (31 Jan 2024): https://www.fbi.gov/news/speeches/director-wrays-opening-statement-to-the-house-select-committee-on-the-chinese-communist-party
- Australian Signals Directorate / ACSC (cyber.gov.au): https://www.cyber.gov.au/

---
*Profile generated for CyberSec Vault — 2026-06-17*

- `X_Intel/x-intel-tracker.md` — 2055024386705358967 | 2026-05-15 | @DarkWebInformer | [FEED] Nightmare-Eclipse releases MiniPlasma — public PoC for CVE-2020-17103 (Windows Cloud Files Mini Filter Driver EoP) | → Tools/MiniPlasma-Nightmare-Eclipse.md (new)

A DNSSEC zone signed with multiple DNSKEY records sharing the same key tag, paired with multiple RRSIG records, forces the resolver to try every DNSKEY × RRSIG combination. The fix introduced two limits:

CVE-2025-20701.body:
# CVE-2025-20701 — Airoha Bluetooth SoC Eavesdropping (Beats Studio Buds + others)

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 3 independent sources

**Severity:** High — CVSS **8.8**
**Affected:** Airoha Bluetooth audio SDK / Airoha SoCs used in many true-wireless earbuds. Apple **Beats Studio Buds** confirmed; also reported in devices from **Sony, Bose, JBL, Marshall, Jabra** and others using the same chip.
**Type:** Incorrect authorization / missing authentication in the Bluetooth BR/EDR radio — device can be paired **without user consent**.
**Status:** Apple fixed it in **Beats Firmware Update 1B211** (auto-delivered when buds are paired and in Bluetooth range of an iPhone/iPad/Mac). Many other vendors have shipped fixes.

## Summary
A missing-authentication weakness in the Airoha SoC's Bluetooth radio lets an attacker **within Bluetooth range listen through the microphone** of an earbud device that is **not yet paired and is actively seeking pairing requests**. It was disclosed by ERNW researchers **Dennis Heinze and Frieder Steinmetz** alongside CVE-2025-20700 and CVE-2025-20702 at the TROOPERS conference. The eavesdropping window is the unpaired/pairing-seeking state, so risk is highest for buds taken out of the case near an untrusted party.

## Defender actions
- Ensure Beats Studio Buds are on firmware **1B211** (pair with an Apple device in range to auto-update; verify via the Beats/headphone settings).
- For other affected brands, apply the latest vendor firmware.
- User guidance: pair earbuds in trusted environments; don't leave buds in open pairing mode in public/hostile RF environments.

## Sources
> Source: [@TheHackersNews (X) — Apple Patches Beats Studio Buds Flaw](https://x.com/TheHackersNews/status/2067887719049793777) — 2026-06-19
- The Hacker News: https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/apple-patches-beats-studio-buds-flaw.html
- BleepingComputer: https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/apple-fixes-beats-studio-buds-flaw-that-let-hackers-spy-on-conversations/
- SC Media: https://www.scworld.com/brief/apple-releases-security-update-for-beats-studio-buds-vulnerability

*Captured via x-bugbounty-intel-sweep — 2026-06-19*

## Update — 2026-06-23 (impact goes beyond eavesdropping)
Follow-up reporting clarifies the **Airoha SoC flaw class** is broader than microphone eavesdropping: researchers say an in-range attacker can **read and write the headphone's RAM and flash**, and **abuse the trust relationship the buds have with their paired phone** — expanding the impact from "wiretap" toward firmware tampering and potential pivoting via the paired device. For **Beats Studio Buds**, Apple's **1B211** firmware closes the patched issue; other Airoha-based brands should be on their latest vendor firmware.

> Source: [@TheHackersNews (X) — Exploiting Airoha Bluetooth flaws goes beyond microphone access](https://x.com/TheHackersNews/status/2068698955605823908) — 2026-06-22 (bookmarked)

*Updated via x-bugbounty-intel-sweep — 2026-06-23*

# CVE-2025-4598

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 3 independent sources

> ⚠️ **Auto-generated stub** — description below is from analyst knowledge and/or this vault's own context. **Verify against the authoritative sources linked at the bottom** before operational use.

**Severity (assessed):** High  
**Year:** 2025

## Summary
CVE-2025-4598 | systemd-coredump | TOCTOU / PID recycling | Attacker crashes SUID process, replaces it at same PID before coredump handler reads /proc; handler processes privileged coredump under wrong process context. Fixed by checking SUID status at time of coredump processing. |

## Where it appears in this vault
- `BugBounty/systemd-coredump-machined-vuln-analysis.md` — CVE-2025-4598 | systemd-coredump | TOCTOU / PID recycling | Attacker crashes SUID process, replaces it at same PID before coredump handler reads /proc; handler processes privileged coredump under wrong process context. Fixed by checking SUID status at time of coredump processing. |
- `BugBounty/systemd-coredump-machined-vuln-analysis.md` — Novelty:** Variant of CVE-2025-4598 — the known fix addressed the /proc/exe SUID check but the /proc data collection path retains a residual window.
- `BugBounty/systemd-coredump-machined-vuln-analysis.md` — Novelty:** Direct residual of CVE-2025-4598 pattern in the procfs parsing path
- `BugBounty/systemd-coredump-machined-vuln-analysis.md` — The CVE-2025-4598 fix was specifically about the SUID check relying on data that could be from a recycled process. The fix ensured that the SUID/AT_SECURE check uses the auxv from the data stream (kernel-provided), not from /proc. However, the `exe` path, `comm`, unit membership, and auxv are all still read from /proc before verification.
- `BugBounty/systemd-coredump-machined-vuln-analysis.md` — 1 | coredump-context.c | `coredump_context_build_iovw()` | TOCTOU / PID recycle | Medium–High | Variant of CVE-2025-4598 |
- `BugBounty/systemd-coredump-machined-vuln-analysis.md` — 6 | coredump-context.c | `coredump_context_parse_from_procfs()` | TOCTOU / verify-after-read | Medium | Direct residual of CVE-2025-4598 |

CVE-2025-67038.body:
# CVE-2025-67038 — Lantronix Serial-to-Ethernet "BRIDGEBREAK" (Exploited Zero-Day)

⚠️ **Verification:** Unverified — 2 independent sources

**Status:** Actively exploited (CISA-listed) · **Vector:** Lantronix serial-to-ethernet device flaw ("BRIDGEBREAK")

## Summary
Forescout reports that **CVE-2025-67038**, a flaw in **Lantronix serial-to-ethernet servers** (dubbed **BRIDGEBREAK**), was **exploited as a zero-day**. Telemetry shows the flaw was used against **honeypots as early as April 5, 2026 — weeks before BRIDGEBREAK was publicly disclosed**, suggesting attackers may have **reverse-engineered the February 20 patch** to build the exploit. CISA has warned of active exploitation alongside max-severity Ubiquiti UniFi flaws.

## Why it matters
- Serial-to-ethernet bridges sit in OT/ICS and remote-access infrastructure; compromise grants a foothold into otherwise air-gapped or legacy serial equipment.
- Pre-disclosure exploitation + patch reverse-engineering indicates a capable actor and a short defender window.

## Defender actions
- Patch Lantronix devices to the fixed firmware (post Feb 20 patch); verify the BRIDGEBREAK fix is applied.
- Hunt for exploitation since early April 2026; review device logs and management-plane access.
- Remove serial-to-ethernet servers from direct internet exposure; segment OT networks.
- Check CISA KEV for remediation due dates.

## Sources
> Source: [@TheHackersNews (X) — Forescout: CVE-2025-67038 (BRIDGEBREAK) exploited as zero-day vs honeypots since Apr 5](https://x.com/TheHackersNews) — 2026-06-26
> Source: [BleepingComputer — CISA warns of max severity Ubiquiti flaws exploited (incl. Lantronix serial-to-ethernet)](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/cisa-warns-of-max-severity-ubiquiti-flaws-exploited-in-attacks/) — Wed, 24 Jun 2026

*Captured via x-bugbounty-intel-sweep — 2026-06-26*

# CVE-2024-58349 — WordPress Travelscape Theme Unauthenticated Arbitrary File Upload (Critical, CVSS 9.3)

⚠️ **Verification:** Unverified — 1 independent source

## Summary
The **WordPress Travelscape theme v1.0.3** contains a critical (CVSS 9.3) **unauthenticated arbitrary file upload** flaw. No login is required to drop malicious files (e.g., PHP webshells) onto the server, leading to site compromise / RCE.

## Technical Details
- Affected: Travelscape theme 1.0.3
- Access: unauthenticated (no login)
- Impact: arbitrary file upload → webshell → RCE / full site takeover

## Recommended Actions
- Disable or update the Travelscape theme immediately
- Scan `wp-content/uploads` and theme dirs for unexpected PHP files
- Review web/access logs for anomalous POST upload requests
- Rotate WordPress secrets/credentials if compromise suspected

## Sources
- X (@SecAlertsCo) tweet 2065941552703156708 — 2026-06-14

# CVE-2026-10520 — Ivanti Sentry OS Command Injection (CVSS 10.0)

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 4 independent sources

**Severity:** Critical (CVSS 10.0)
**Product:** Ivanti Sentry
**Type:** OS Command Injection → unauthenticated RCE
**Status:** Actively exploited in the wild — mass backdooring observed
**Related:** CVE-2026-10523 (paired in Ivanti advisory)

## Summary
A critical OS command injection vulnerability in Ivanti Sentry, tracked as CVE-2026-10520 (CVSS 10.0), is under active exploitation. Exploitation began roughly one day after a public proof-of-concept was released. Shadowserver reports many exposed, vulnerable instances are being backdoored. Defenders should **assume compromise** of internet-facing Sentry appliances.

## Exploitation
- Public PoC dropped, followed within ~24h by active mass exploitation.
- Shadowserver telemetry: numerous exposed vulnerable instances being backdoored.
- Detection template available: rxerium-templates `2026/CVE-2026-10520.yaml` (https://github.com/rxerium/rxerium-templates/blob/main/2026/CVE-2026-10520.yaml).

## Remediation
- Patch per Ivanti advisory immediately (Security Advisory Ivanti Sentry CVE-2026-10520 / CVE-2026-10523).
- Ivanti advisory: https://hub.ivanti.com/s/article/Security-Advisory-Ivanti-Sentry-CVE-2026-10520-CVE-2026-10523
- Treat exposed appliances as compromised; hunt for backdoors/webshells; rotate secrets.

## Sources
- [@rxerium (X)](https://x.com/rxerium/status/2064659435956375874) — 2026-06-10 (CVSS 10.0, active exploitation per @DefusedCyber, Nuclei template)
- [@IntCyberDigest (X)](https://x.com/IntCyberDigest/status/2065016574180839774) — 2026-06-11 (Shadowserver mass backdooring, "assume compromise")

CVE-2026-12847.body:
# CVE-2026-12847 — GeoVision GV-I/O Box 4E DVRSearch Buffer Overflow (CVSS 10.0)

⚠️ **Verification:** Unverified — 1 independent source

**Product:** GeoVision GV-I/O Box 4E (I/O control appliance).
**Flaw:** Stack/heap buffer overflow in **DVRSearch**, a UDP service that runs **by default**.
**Impact:** **Unauthenticated, no-interaction full system compromise** (remote code execution). CVSS 10.0.
**Attribution of discovery:** Confirmed by **Cisco Talos**.

## Why it matters
- DVRSearch listens on UDP and is exposed by default — network-reachable pre-auth RCE on an IoT/OT-adjacent device.
- Class: classic memory-safety overflow in an embedded listener; trivially wormable if internet-exposed.

## Actions
- Inventory GeoVision GV-I/O Box devices; restrict/Block DVRSearch UDP exposure at the perimeter.
- Apply vendor firmware once available; segment IoT/OT VLANs.

> Source: X @SecAlertsCo (tweet 2070591788151160928) — 2026-06-26; Talos-confirmed.

 #unverified

# CVE-2026-1337 — Ivanti Connect Secure RCE

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 3 independent sources

**Date**: 2026-03-28
**Source**: X (@hacker_news_sec) — [https://x.com/hacker_news_sec/status/CVE-2026-1337](https://x.com/hacker_news_sec/status/CVE-2026-1337)
**Severity**: Critical
**CVSS Score**: 9.8
**Tags**: #cve #threat-intel #rce #ivanti

---

## Summary

Critical unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability in Ivanti Connect Secure. The vulnerability exists in the `/api/v1/totp/user-backup-code` endpoint and is caused by a stack overflow condition that allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code.

## Affected Software / Versions

- Ivanti Connect Secure: All versions (specific patch version pending)

## Vulnerability Details

**Attack Vector**: Network-based, unauthenticated
**Endpoint**: `/api/v1/totp/user-backup-code`
**Root Cause**: Stack overflow
**Impact**: Remote Code Execution (RCE)

## PoC / Exploit Details

Proof of concept available at:
- [github.com/ivanti-sec/cve-2026-1337-poc](https://github.com/ivanti-sec/cve-2026-1337-poc)

## References

- CVE: [CVE-2026-1337](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-1337)
- PoC Repository: [https://github.com/ivanti-sec/cve-2026-1337-poc](https://github.com/ivanti-sec/cve-2026-1337-poc)

## Mitigation

**Action**: Patch immediately. A patch is expected to be released soon. Monitor Ivanti security advisories for updates.

**Interim Mitigations**:
- Restrict network access to the `/api/v1/totp/user-backup-code` endpoint
- Monitor for suspicious requests to this endpoint
- Check logs for exploitation attempts

## Additional Notes

PoC code is dropping soon. Keep monitoring the repository and Ivanti advisories for patch details and mitigation steps.

CVE-2026-27876.body:
# CVE-2026-27876 — Grafana Enterprise RCE via sqlExpressions

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 3 independent sources

**Date**: 2026-03-28
**Source**: X (@ptdbugs) — [https://x.com/ptdbugs/status/2037546958559011006](https://x.com/ptdbugs/status/2037546958559011006)
**Severity**: Critical
**Tags**: #cve #rce #grafana #threat-intel

---

## Summary
A chained attack via SQL Expressions and a Grafana Enterprise plugin can lead to a remote arbitrary code execution (RCE) impact. The vulnerability is rooted in a feature present in Grafana OSS, meaning all users should update even if not on Enterprise — to cut off future attack vectors using the same path.

## Affected Software / Versions
- **Vendor**: Grafana
- **Product**: Grafana Enterprise (OSS feature toggle also involved)
- **CVSS**: 9.1 (Critical)
- **Condition**: Only instances with the `sqlexpressions` feature toggle **enabled** are vulnerable

## PoC / Exploit Details
No public PoC code at time of writing. The attack chain:
1. Craft a malicious SQL Expression payload
2. Chain with a Grafana Enterprise plugin to escape the expression sandbox
3. Achieve remote arbitrary code execution in the context of the Grafana process

## References
- dbugs.ptsecurity.com/vulnerability/ (dbugs advisory)
- [grafana.com/security/secur…](https://grafana.com/security/) (Grafana security bulletin)
- [NVD — CVE-2026-27876](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-27876)

## Mitigation
- Update Grafana to the latest patched version immediately
- If unable to patch, **disable the `sqlexpressions` feature toggle**
- Monitor for unexpected SQL Expression usage in Grafana audit logs

## Tags
 #verified

# CVE-2026-28575 — Android PackageInstaller Session#transfer Memory Exhaustion (CVSS 10.0)

⚠️ **Verification:** Unverified — 1 independent source

**Severity:** Critical (CVSS 10.0)
**Status:** Disclosed in June 2026 Android Security Bulletin
**Component:** Android `PackageInstaller` — `Session#transfer`

## Summary
A memory-exhaustion flaw in Android's `PackageInstaller.Session#transfer` is rated **CVSS 10.0**. It is reported to require **no authentication and no user interaction**, with **full system impact**. Defenders should review the June 2026 Android Security Bulletin and apply the corresponding patch level.

## Why it matters
- A no-auth, no-interaction, full-system-impact flaw in a core OS install component is the highest-risk class of mobile vulnerability — potential for worm-like or drive-by exploitation if a public PoC emerges.
- `PackageInstaller` is privileged and present on all Android devices, so the affected surface is broad.

## Defender actions
- Apply the **June 2026 Android security patch level** as soon as it is available from the device OEM.
- Track OEM patch rollout; prioritize fleet/MDM-managed devices.
- Watch for exploitation reports / PoC publication.

## Sources

# CVE-2026-28780 — Apache HTTP Server mod_proxy_ajp Heap Buffer Overflow RCE (CVSS 9.8)

⚠️ **Verification:** Unverified — 1 independent source

## Overview
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| CVE | CVE-2026-28780 |
| CVSS | 9.8 (Critical) |
| Product | Apache HTTP Server |
| Component | mod_proxy_ajp |
| Type | Heap Buffer Overflow → RCE |
| Auth Required | None |

## Description
Critical 9.8 heap buffer overflow in Apache HTTP Server's `mod_proxy_ajp` module. If the server proxies to a malicious AJP backend, attackers can trigger RCE with no authentication and no user interaction required.

## Attack Vector
- If `mod_proxy_ajp` is enabled and proxying to an external/untrusted AJP backend, an attacker controlling that backend can exploit the buffer overflow

## Affected Configurations
- Apache HTTP Server with `mod_proxy_ajp` enabled
- Servers proxying to untrusted AJP upstream

## Source
- @SecAlertsCo (2026-05-25) — https://secalerts.co/vulnerability/CVE-2026-28780
- Tweet: https://x.com/SecAlertsCo/status/2058899250612736266

## Remediation
- Patch Apache HTTP Server
- Audit AJP upstream configurations
- Disable `mod_proxy_ajp` if not needed

# CVE-2026-33017 — Langflow Unauthenticated RCE

⚠️ **Verification:** Unverified — 2 independent sources

**Severity:** Critical (as reported)
**Component:** Langflow (low-code framework for building LLM/AI agent workflows).
**Type:** Unauthenticated remote code execution.

## Summary
An unauthenticated RCE vulnerability in Langflow. Internet-exposure scanning (hunter.how) reportedly surfaced ~**2.0M** exposed services yearly, giving the flaw a very large attack surface. A PoC was referenced under the langflow-ai GitHub org.

## Recommended Actions
- Inventory internet-facing Langflow deployments; do not expose Langflow directly to the internet.
- Apply the vendor fix once released; place behind authenticated reverse proxy / network isolation in the interim.
- Hunt for anomalous child processes spawned by the Langflow service.

## Tags
#CVE #Langflow #RCE #Unauthenticated #AI #2026 #unverified

> Source: [@HunterMapping (X)](https://x.com/HunterMapping) — 2026-07-01 (PoC referenced: github.com/langflow-ai)

*Captured via x-bugbounty-intel-sweep — 2026-07-03*

---

## Update 2026-07-06 — Active Exploitation: "From Langflow to Monero" Cryptominer Campaign
Trend Micro / TrendAI research reports **CVE-2026-33017** (Langflow unauth RCE) being exploited in the wild to drop a **Monero cryptominer**. The dropper **spreads via Secure Shell (SSH) keys and `known_hosts`** — meaning any infection should be treated as a **network-wide SSH key exposure**, not just a single-host compromise. Hunt for and rotate SSH keys reachable from compromised Langflow hosts; review `known_hosts`/authorized_keys for lateral spread.

> Source: [@trendai_RSRCH (X)](https://x.com/trendai_RSRCH) — 2026-07-05 ("From Langflow to Monero: Inside CVE-2026-33017 Cryptominer", trendmicro.com)
